0:00:04 Speaker 1: Meet Kevin Harrington.
0:00:05 Speaker 2: With over 500 products launched and generating over $5 billion in product sales
in over 100 countries, Kevin Harrington is the Inventor of the Infomercial. As seen on TV Pioneer, and

Billy Mays, Paula Abdul, and Tony Little.
0:00:36 Speaker 3: My brand now is worth over $3 billion, so my advice to everybody, if you even
have an idea, you’d wanna go to Kevin Harrington.
0:00:44 Speaker 2: As a sought­after guest, Kevin has been featured on 20/20, CNN, Fox Business,

The Today Show, CNBC Squawk Box, Entrepreneur, Fox and Friends, Donny Deutsch Big Idea,

0:01:03 Kevin Harrington: Now back to the Shark Tank, I’m Kevin Harrington.
0:01:06 Speaker 2: A renowned speaker, Kevin has appeared on stage for organizations like AT&T,
Microsoft, United States Postal Service, Humana, 3M, Fedex, Auburn University, Harvard, and
MIT. Kevin has authored several books including Act Now!: How I Turned Ideas into Million Dollar
Products, and the bestseller, How to Become a Key Person of Influence. As a global influencer, Kevin
has thrown out the first pitch for the Tampa Bay Rays and produced movies with stars like Kevin
Spacey in the Father of Invention.
0:01:35 Speaker 5: Kevin is a pioneer for the direct response industry and has risen to nearly iconic
status over the years.
0:01:41 Speaker 2: Kevin helped to found EO, the Entrepreneurs Organization, and ERA, the
Electronic Retailing Association, receiving their lifetime achievement award. Kevin is now at the
forefront of the digital marketing age as founder of Quantum Media, a cutting edge digital marketing
agency. Kevin has built 20 businesses to over $100 million each. That’s why he’s the original Shark
from Shark Tank. The Billion Dollar Man, Kevin Harrington. Let’s give Kevin a warm welcome.

were doing hundreds of millions of dollars, yes, but this was the old way, and I say today, there’s a
much smarter way. And this is why I have to say that employees aren’t as engaged today as they
used to be.
0:17:10 KH: And so do I want to hire a bunch of people? Millennials are the least engaged, 17.5%
of people hate their jobs, right? So what I say is, Wall Street Journal just did this study, and this was

outsource every job we can that isn’t customer­facing. And so Google, which is huge, they do half
of their people are outsourced, half are employees. And they spend 300 million a year with just one
outside firm so... And what’s happening now in the world? There’s this trend towards what I call the
gig and the YouEconomy, part­time jobs. When you... Let’s think about it. Look at the...
Freelancer.com, Zipcar, Uber, TaskRabbit, right?
0:18:00 KH: Job hopping is the new norm. 50% of the US workforce is gonna be employed in the

“I’m gonna rent in existing infrastracture.” Right? “And I’m gonna use virtual services, partner where
possible, and don’t invest in expensive overhead.” And what company did he found? He founded
Snapchat, alright? He rented... Evan Spiegel, a multibillionaire now. He rented Amazon’s Cloud. They
got all... Amazon’s got all the money. They put up the money to develop the cloud, and then he went
to Square for the financial side of it, partnered with Square, partnered with Amazon. And he had a $4
billion company with 20 employees. And now obviously, just went public recently, $25 billion, right? So

scaling, he was free to focus on growing the business.
0:19:25 KH: Business development and that is the key, so he could 10X his company, this is the

you utilize other people’s assets, partner where possible, and get other people to leverage your

owners in America, and they’re all like, “Oh, the internet’s killing us, we’re being crushed and being
put out of business ‘cause these guys are selling stuff on the internet.” And I’m like, “Well, that’s
because they’re taking your customers because you have no digital strategy, okay?”
0:31:37 KH: And so, for me, it started happening about three or four years ago when I looked at... I

can, okay? This almost destroyed my business, I own asseenontv.com, 2000 products in the As Seen

gotta come up with a digital format.”

KEVIN HARRINGTON
0:32:25 KH: So this is what we’ve been focused on, because millennials are ditching their TV sets.

0:35:30 KH: So yeah, these are the trends that are happening, obviously, TV’s down, mobile and

a program where based on what you bought... They’re testing this already, they’re gonna just start

something that we just sent you, we’ll just charge your card in a week or so.” And so they’re having

things that some of the really smart marketers’ doing. The other thing is heat mapping of websites,
finding out where all your traffic is, and again some of this stuff gets a little technical, I don’t want to
bore you with too much detail, but you can not only heat map a website to see where is the traffic...
0:36:47 KH: If they’re not going to one part of your website at all, maybe you got a problem with your
website. But also in the stores, you can heat map the store itself. And a lot of the retailers that are
having issues with traffic are finding out they got parts of the store that nobody’s going to.
They’re over­built, way too much space, so now there’s a trend towards smaller types of space. So
this is a couple of the trends, and one more little trend. This is called geo­fencing. We went to the
Miami Boat Show recently, just real close to here, and we geo­fenced the entire trade show, and then
we were able to do push notifications into all the mobile phones inside that show. And we had two
clients that we represented that we’ve crushed, sending people to their booth. So this is something
very, very powerful and successful, being used by smart marketers now, and some of the techniques
that we’re taking a look at also.
0:37:47 KH: One last part of this section, how many have heard of the Internet of Things? A few of

it’s internet­connected thermostat that monitors your living habits. Where you go, what you do, what
time you wake up, what time you leave the house. And it adjusts the temperature of the heat and the
air conditioning based on it learning your living habits. And so from plugs to feeders to light bulbs,
everything across the board is turning into internet. In fact, if you want to see some crazy ones, how
about a toothbrush for moms that know when the kids are lying about how long they’re brushing their
teeth? How about that one? Or a belt that loosens when you’ve eaten too much? So there are... Right
now in the average home today, about eight internet­connected devices. When you think about it, your
phone, your computer, your IPad, whatever. And by the year 2020, it’s gonna be 50 internet­connected
devices in your home, so there’s a huge opportunity.
0:38:51 KH: Me as a marketer, what do I say? This is why I go to the Consumer Electronics Show,

because we look for micro moments in the marketing world. And the reason being is that the average
person checks their phone 150 times a day, they’re on it 177 minutes a day, and they consult their
phone 82% of the time when they’re shopping. So how do you reach these people at these micro
moments?
0:39:48 KH: I just went to a property and casualty insurance group, and I said, “You have to reach

phone, right?” So this is the important thing, is knowing how and when to reach them, and that’s
12

KEVIN HARRINGTON
what digital is all about. And this is why John Wanamaker said, “Half the money I spend on marketing

world of digital, and I’m just gonna rifle through the five because when Russell gets up I’m sure he’s
gonna really focus on this. But when we meet a client we say, “What’s you goal? Do you want to sell
products? Do you want to do lead generation?” We do a little audit on their website, check out what
they’re doing. And then social is very important. So what networks are you on? Are you on Facebook,
Pinterest, Instagram? And now when you think about Snapchat and Instagram, it’s millennials,
organic, unbranded lifestyle, whereas Facebook is the mass market. And it’s really gearing up
towards women right now, and Pinterest is fashion and beauty. So don’t try to do it all, kinda zero in
and focus on a couple of the key ones.
0:41:12 KH: So how many have heard of Squatty Potty? The little stool that you put your legs in, it

see... I hear what the average American watches TV 4.3 hours a day, I go, “How in the hell did
they get that time?” Five nights a week I go to school. I’m always... I think a man is as alive as he’s
learning. Once you think you learned it all, you’re dead. Okay? So you got to keep doing that.
37:37 TC: Lastly, who do you help? Guys, when you get to become successful, you’ve got to

07:54 JJ: The guy that founded Chick-fil-A died in 2014. He talked about the culture, the culture was

JACK “JJ” JACKINTELLE
So be clear about your mission. Another one is very important, clear performance standards, very

10:55 JJ: I’m a car dealer. I changed what a first time salesman needs to sell. “You need to sell 10

Volkswagen store the month we open. Are you okay with that? Do you believe in that? And then we’re
gonna be a number one Volkswagen store every month after.” So number two was non­negotiable.
The general manager of that store knew if we weren’t number one, because we were very clear. It

for your people. It’s fair to them, it’s fair to you, and it’s enforceable. You leave it gray, you’re wide
open. So I think it’s very, very important of one of the five primary components to your core values and
your culture to be clear about what you expect.

What a washed out way of implementing your culture. “No, it’s nine the first month. That’s what we

13:58 JJ: The key to culture, they must share your values, believe in your mission, have the

desk everyday, we created a culture card. It has our core competencies in it. It has our mission. It’s

15:36 JJ: The very last thing that has to happen. My human resource, Theron Stokes, walks in my

read the culture card. It isn’t about how many cars they sold, how may cars they service, what their

or can convince me in 60 seconds based on reading the culture card. And I tell you what, it’s a real

company. I completely feel like you’re sincere on what I saw on your video, thank you very much.” And

day, my role changed six years ago. I went from implementing process to inspecting the culture of the

18:51 JJ: I’m telling you, strengthen your culture foundation strikes at the root of the sustainability

That crappy cracked up foundation until a good wind comes by. Until the economy dips. Those are
the dealers that went out of business 660 some odd dealers went out of business in ‘08. They were

20:08 JJ: You ever seen this team go to the Super Bowl with a bunch of trick plays? Good teams,

perform that produces results year after year after year. Quit trying to run trick plays in your business.

22:28 JJ: When Grant decided to fill this room with 2200 people, it was another floor on the

eyes and go, “Really?” And then it wouldn’t have succeeded because at this point you gotta quit being

25:21 JJ: Visible everyday. You wanna talk to him, walk right up on you. Say, “What’re you wearing

hey bud, you got some food on your lip.” ‘Cause people will just look at him. And guess what, they

26:29 JJ: Maybe you don’t bite off a skyscraper that goes up a 1000 floors and has a foundation the

JACK “JJ” JACKINTELLE
built over time. I just said that, not overnight. Wise leaders accelerate their efforts by eliminating

Creating a stronger benchmark what is expected in terms of performance and number will dictate the
behavior, you’ll see that. You’ll see, you start to become visible, you change your role as a leader, you

27:31 JJ: Three key merit culture traits. The following concepts must be woven into the vocabulary
standard and the culture of any great company. Earn, big word, we’ll talk about it. I believe in earning,
which you get. I believe in paying people what they’re worth. A big mistake a lot of us make as leaders
as it relates to earn is how many of us have given somebody a raise when they quit? What a terrible
way to operate your business. Give them the damn raise when they earn it. Don’t give them the raise
when they don’t earn it.
[applause]
28:11 JJ: Bring somebody into your office and say, “Guess what, man, you are killing it,” because if
you don’t do it, somebody else is gonna give them what they earn, you’re gonna lose them or you’re
gonna be chasing them. Give them what they earn, don’t give them what they don’t earn, and you
can live that way if you’re clear, you’re transparent and you’re fair. “Why didn’t I get a raise? I’ve been
here three years, Tommy’s been here nine months.” He earned it. And that’s the way I operate. I
operate a company based on merit, I don’t care how long you’ve been here, I’m gonna give you what
you earn. Deserve, primarily the same thing.
[applause]
28:46 JJ: To be worth what you qualified for. That goes for every individual here in the room too.
Don’t expect anything you didn’t deserve. You know whether you deserved it or not, don’t walk into
the office and ask your boss for a raise when you know you didn’t deserve it, just because someone
else did. But if you deserve it, go get it. If somebody deserves it in your company give it to them,
be that guy. What do you want people to say about you when you’re not in the room? “That’s a guy
that’s clear, he’s transparent, he will give you what you’re worth, he won’t pay you if you’re not.” I
think responsible is a big one. To be the primary cause of something. Negative or positive, someone
is responsible for everything that happened. Who’s responsible? Should I take care of him? Should I
talk to him? Should I have reprimanded him? Everyone needs to understand they’re responsible for
their actions. These are three key merit culture traits that’ve gotta be woven into every single day of
what you do. If you are a company of one, be responsible, take responsibility. We’re gonna talk about
scapegoating in a minute, take responsibility. If you’ve earned it, go ask for it. If you deserve it, get it.
Whether you’re delegating that out and you’re doing it for your people or it’s you individually.
30:05 JJ: Ultimately, if it’s you individually, I met some car salesmen in here today, when you become
41

a manager, you’ll take that same thing, when you earned it, you got it. If they won’t give it you, go
somewhere else where you can get it and then when you get to that place you’re going, give it to the
people that earn it. It will be woven into your culture forever. Getting tough to do this. This culture and
culture right is about being great. I’m here today about making you great. Not giving you something
to talk about. I’m talking about being great, getting great. I’m sure everybody here is expecting to
build a huge skyscraper when they get here. You’ve gotta be tough. You’ve gotta care enough. And
I’ll tell you, what an empowering thing to do. You’ve gotta care enough to turn around and remove
culture infections in your company. Not just care enough about your company, but care enough about
the people that are there doing things the right way. People depend on us as leaders to walk in and
eliminate these infections in our company. It’s a tough thing to do, man. I’ve had to fire my best friend.
I’ve had to fire people I care deeply about. I’ve had to fire people and terminate people with nine kids.
But I was fair, prior to that, I was clear, I was transparent. I have never sat down with anybody and
terminated them and they said, “Wow! I never saw that coming.” Never!
31:35 JJ: That’s something I take pride in. Oh you saw it coming. You knew when I walked in the
room where we were gonna be because I told you what it was gonna take to get the job done. I
told you what the goals were. I told you what the culture was, so you knew when I walked in here
today. You weren’t the number one Volkswagen dealer, you’re number two now. That was not the
benchmark we set, we gotta bust a move, man. So you gotta be tough. It’s fair to your people. It’s fair
to all the other people depending on you to be tough. Zero tolerance. This is no area you can play.
Fair. You can be tough with a smile. I’ve never yelled and screamed at anybody to make a move.
People will appreciate that. The problem with the way we run our business is well, we let human
resources dictate everything because we haven’t clearly explored what we can get away with and
how we can run our businesses. So you sit down with this poor guy and you tell him, “We’re just
making some changes.” Goes home to his wife and he says, “What happened today?” “Well, they’re
just making some changes.” You’ve done him an injustice, you’ve done your company an injustice. If
you started it the right way, he goes home and says, “I didn’t live up to the expectations of what it was
gonna take to get the job. I gotta get my act together. I got three kids.” It’s how you wanna live your
life. That’s the type of culture you want to be associated with.
32:55 JJ: Let’s talk about some other infections as it relates to hurting your culture. To red tape, if

your standards are infectious. You let them hang around, they will infect it. Not only will they infect it
by their behavior, what they’re doing but will also infect the other people that are involved. They will
infect the other people’s standard of, “Well, he’s doing it and he’s still here.” So getting tough, making
sure that you eliminate these infections is critical.
33:40 JJ: The following concepts must be weeded out of the vocabulary standards and the culture

entitled. I’m not entitled and no one that works for you is entitled to everything. Go back to the slides,
42

JACK “JJ” JACKINTELLE
four slides ago, they’ve gotta earn it. Entitlement’s a killer. No one’s entitled to anything.
They gotta earn it, run a company based on merit. Mediocre, you wanna be great, probably not the
place for you to sit today. I don’t assume anybody in here wants to be the mediocre. Don’t accept
mediocracy. There’s a bunch of companies that will. But they’re companies that aren’t here today.
Mediocre’s gotta be something you just cannot tolerate. It cannot be talked about. We don’t do things
mediocre. I don’t, you don’t, we can’t. It’s about being great. This isn’t about running an average
company. You guys are here because I assume you want to be great.
34:38 JJ: Excuses, a plea offered for a fault or failure. You do excuses or you don’t, bottom line.
There’s always an excuse. There’s always a reason. Isn’t it funny that the people who are late to
work are always the ones that get in traffic? It just becomes a way of living. If you’re a company of
one today, all of these things apply to you. If you’re a car salesman, don’t make excuses. Don’t be
mediocre. Don’t feel entitled. If you’re one guy here with no associates, it applies the same, don’t
accept excuses. I’ve never been late. I can tell you, I’ve never been late. What do I just never hit
traffic? I’m not late. People that are late seem to always have their traffic because they make the
excuses. People who don’t hit their benchmarks have excuses. It’s gotta be eliminated, eliminated to
be great. If you start to accept them, you’re never gonna be great.
[applause]
35:40 JJ: You’ve established this foundation, you’ve gone back to the office, you’ve laid it in. This is
a growing, growing, growing piece of your business. It’s moving, it’s changing. It’s a garden, it’s got
weeds coming up every day. We don’t pluck them weeds, you don’t take care of it, it will take over. If
you fail to shape your culture, your image, society will do it for you, worse than ever right now. Society
will take over. You stay in the back office, you don’t do it, if you’re a company of one, you buy into the
propaganda you see on social media and so on, you’ll start to believe your own nonsense. Society
will shape your culture for you, eventually ruin your company, your success, get in the way of it. With
its mindset, its influences, its trends, we’ve got a wimpy society, people. People love excuses, they
feel entitled. So, you’ve got to take control of your culture before society does, or you’ll be wiped
out. The next recession’s coming, it’s just a matter of when. You wanna be great, take control of it,
understand it. You can’t just do it once, it’s gotta change the way you do things everyday. You’re a
company of one, don’t buy into that stuff because we all can let ourselves off the hook.
36:54 JJ: Four primary society influences, things society influences that will influence your company.
A decline in civility, you know what that means? People aren’t nice, something that will hurt your
company will create problems throughout and it’s just a nasty way of doing business, demand people
to be nice, people don’t think they need to be nice anymore. Demand a nice culture. I want people to
be civil. I want them to help each other. I want them to go to coffee pot and say, “Can I get you a cup
of coffee?” I want to be empathetic when people have issues. I want them to cover for people. Society
does not speak of that. People think it’s okay to be not nice, it will ruin your company, it will eat at your
culture, eventually put you out of business. A rise in entitlements, stop it immediately, nip it the second
it comes up. If I hear about somebody being negative in my company, I will go find them. If I feel or
43

I hear somebody feeling entitled, I will go square up with them, and let them know absolutely not,
whether it’s a manager, no matter who it is, there is no entitlement.
[applause]
38:04 JJ: Normalized... Again, these are influences from society, this is what they’re seeing, more
than ever, the new social media, their friends, all these unemployed people, all these people that are
okay with mediocracy. Normalized immortality. You know what that means? It means, “I don’t need to
get it done today, it’s gonna be fine. I’m gonna live forever. I don’t need to do this now. I’m immortal.”
Today, we don’t do immortal, we do today. Normalized mortality is something that people are living,
it’s okay. I’ll make them calls tomorrow. So, understand that society is influencing this.
38:45 JJ: Man, this is a good one, the scapegoating of successful people. Shoot ‘em in the head,

education than me.” All of that nonsense. Scapegoating, and first of all, again, if you’re a company of

in the car with them, you listen when they’re talking, you go to lunch with them, didn’t just happen,
may happen for a minute when they do a tent sale but if they’re successful, you don’t scapegoat
anything. I don’t care what their background was or where they came from, or what their education
was, you are where you are, this is where you started, so you start scapegoating successful people,
you end up being nothing. These four primary society driven behaviors will destroy your culture if
you let it. They’re considerable and they are persistent threats to your organization, its health, and its
culture. You can’t do it once, you’ve gotta change the way you do things, the way you look at things,
the way you run your business, and you gotta understand everyday there’s a decline in people being
nice.
40:06 JJ: Normalized entitlements, normalized immortality, every single day, you’ve gotta inspect
these things. Gotta commit for the long haul. You wanna be [40:16] successful, again, there’s
no quick fix. I think everybody here knows that or you wouldn’t be here today, you know this thing isn’t
about a quick fix. What I try to establish here this morning, and I’m so glad I got to speak early, was
to give you an idea, how to take all of this awesome information that you’re gonna obtain over these
next three days, and before you try to go build a skyscraper, you identify the foundation of it. You
identify how much load it will hold. And if you can’t put it all in place tomorrow, you table it, then you
build phase two, then you build phase three, you continue to expand. I assure you that the Cardone
Company 10 years ago, I don’t know enough, could not have put this together, could not have
handled the load of this unbelievable conference. He didn’t have the infrastructure, the foundation to
do it.
[applause]
41:18 JJ: But when he decided to do it, he knew the foundation was there. He walked in, said, “Guys,

here’s what we’re gonna do. We’re gonna throw a conference. We’re gonna have 20 some 100

41:58 JJ: I’ll leave you with one final thought, depending on what your business is, your relationship,

can destroy an organization. These two believe in the same things, that’s why they’re successful.
Multiple cultures under one roof. They share the same culture. If you’ve got a business partner that
thinks this way, you think the other way, what are your people gonna do? Poor people. Multiple
cultures under one roof will destroy your organization. Make sure you’re in bed with the right people,
make sure you’re in bed with the right partner, make sure you’re spending your time, business­wise,
personal­wise, of people that believe in your culture. Knock them dead this week. Thank you very
much for listening. Thank you.

18:01 BC: We went from $250 to $600 million. Because it convinced us we were just here, we

go ahead improve this asset, I put the new sidewalks, I pave the parking, I upgrade the kitchens, and
I own these 200 units, I can increase the rents from where it’s at now, another $200. That’s a value
add. So your revenue goes up, your cash flow goes up, the asset appreciation goes up. What’s great
about life, this is the most amazing thing about life, life has a value add. There’s no cap. The more

80% of your hour, that’s what the stats say. 80% of your hour is wasted on non­dividend exchange,

26:27 BC: If you’re a partner and you’re looking to buy out your schlep partner because he doesn’t

own the joint. So, can you just talk about the pluses and the minuses?
40:30 Speaker 5: Well, definitely the pluses are the level of responsibility and the support. I work for
a company that’s 55 years plus, so established. There’s a lot to be said about the experience that
the company brings to the table, and the stability. The brand was established before I got there. The
opportunity to change the culture and do the things that I did were paramount in what’s happened in
the last six, seven years. By far, the disadvantages as it relates to my presentation, I’ve got a founder
that is a traditionalist. He’s in his mid­70’s. We butt heads on culture. We butt heads on certain things
like that, so it does take away from your opportunity sometimes to exercise 100%. My most frustrating
days where I can’t implement a 1,000% of my vision. That comes with working for somebody else.
So, there’s a level of protection, and security that’s there. The right fit is critical to making that work.
And I think that the closer you get to sharing the same vision, and the same culture, the more
successful that piece of it becomes. And the other piece of it is, adding to what they’ve already
developed. No one makes it work, and normally for somebody else, if you don’t come on and take it
to another level. Normally it’s short-lived. The bench marks are pretty clear, which is cool.

45:53 BC: You gotta remember, if you’re calling on some business owners, and you’re calling on an

51:15 Speaker 2: JJ?
51:16 Speaker 5: In the industry we’re in, turnovers are big, I think on the sales force, it’s almost 52%

type of A­plan but I hear all the time, “I hate investing the money in training because I have so much
turnover.” I’ve realized the more training, the more I train them, the more I have them dependent on
the training for their success, the less turnover. So it’s definitely the chicken or the egg. Invest in the
training... I did some research when I did this presentation a couple of years ago on the turnover
between McDonald’s and Chick-fil-A. And Chick-fil-A was like 60% better than McDonald’s. The
people feel trained, they know their job, they know what’s expected of them. And I’m just talking
65

about fast food, which has the highest level of turnover than any industry, is fast food, because no
one intends to stay there. Now I could take you through the whole Chick-fil-A model of management
and college and the things that they do, but training is critical to minimizing turnover. And people don’t
wanna invest in training because they don’t wanna train people because they’re gonna quit, is how
they think. Really, really bad way to think.
52:31 GC: Training?

53:31 BC: We look at our staff, no matter what department they’re in, they have to be a profitable
staff member. You have to produce a net profit. So we look at each individual as a unit. We’re to
the point where we have so many scorecards, we know exactly on a given minute where those
benchmarks are at. And unfortunately, the ones that don’t make it are asked to leave or they part
ways. So it’s up to the business owner or entrepreneur. You can either make them profitable or you
can accept you’re losing money, so that’s how we look at. I don’t know how it is in the car business.

06:49 Speaker 1: Here is a statistic. What’s a Broadway guy doing on stage at a business
convention? Well, it’s called show business. It’s not called show­show. And what is forgotten many
times by people in my industry is that they actually have to do something to get to where they wanna
go. I’m not selling cars. I’m not selling microphones. I’m not selling water. I am my product. I am my
product. So when I walk into a room, if I don’t take care of my product, my chances of survival and
success are minimized. Sleep, work... So when you heard when I got to that high note, I didn’t have
it today. My own fault, my own responsibility, right? Here is a statistic. Actors Equity Association is the
union that governs all professional stage actors, all the big Broadway shows. 69%... How about this?
69% of all members of Actors Equity make $15,000 a year or less. The top 91%, $50,000 a year or
less before taxes. That’s $38,000 after taxes. To live in Manhattan, you are looking at a one bedroom
apartment, average of $3,800 a month rent. Only 1% make over a hundred thousand dollars a year
and it’s a very volatile industry.
08:20 Speaker 1: So, what I teach is I teach artists how to thrive because it’s not taught in schools.
You may be proficient in your industry. When you get to my level, everybody’s got game. Everybody’s
got game. They’re at a very, very, very high level of skill. But what will set you apart and what will
allow you to stick to and through the attrition rate... Attrition, when things start to drop off. Your
competitors will start to drop off, if you last long enough. So, when I look at you, guys, I say you are
your product even if you are selling a car, even if you are a doctor or a dentist doing a root canal.
Because when you walk in that door or when your patient walks in the door or when your client walks
in the door or when you are trying to close somebody on a sale, the first thing they are gonna see is
you. That’s what they are seeing. So, how do you create the star power in your own life? How do you
become the star in your industry?
09:27 Speaker 1: So what I teach and after I read the 10X rule, I realized something. I wasn’t doing
enough. I got to a very high level and then I had a massive fall. Got wiped out literally and I could
have given up. I could have just said, “I’m done. I’m gonna do something else.” But for some reason
within me, I couldn’t. I have two kids. I have a wife. This is what I know. So what I decided to do is I
decided to create those things which will allow me to reach my goals again and then surpass them.
So one of the things I teach, when I ask people this question, I say, “Do you have a dream?” How
many people have a dream here? Alright. How many people have achieved it? There you go. The
question is, why not? Why not? What is stopping you from achieving your dream? Fear? Number one
answer in my surveys. Second? Economy. So, when I ask artists this question, I say, “Why aren’t you
getting to where you wanna go?” “Well, because I don’t have enough money. I had to get a job.”

now. And the results were instantaneous. The followers started going, the haters started flowing, the

see. I’m concerned about price, maybe, “Is this the right feature benefit?” But in back crawl the
90% underneath the water is “I gotta put my name on this thing”, right? “If I install this software for
example how does that effect my operations? Now I have to retrain people”, I can go on and on with
a list of what I’m thinking and most people don’t address that. They think it’s all about feature benefit,
advantage and price. I’m saying that most people are afraid, that part of the brain...

[chuckle] So guys, this is the things I do. I’m gonna give you ideas, okay? Every single customer gets
a handwritten thank you card. Every single customer gets a follow up phone call, okay? Things that
we do culturally, I reward my employees, and motivate my employees to do things that are based,
not around selling, but around cultural customer loyalty. For example, we have a meeting every
month, and that every month we have two awards. One is unsolicited handshake award, so you know
you’re doing a great job with customers if you’re talking to them face to face, like maybe in a retail
environment or whatever, and they turn around to you after you’ve already finished the conversation
and say, “Hey, dude, thank you so much, man, I really appreciate it. It really, really, really was helpful.”
You’re doing it right.
26:19 Speaker 1: Okay, so in our retail stores, we have a handshake award, so there’s a competition
in the retail store of who gets the most unsolicited handshakes, alright? And the guy who gets the
most unsolicited handshakes gets $1,000 at the end of the month in the company. Alright, so it’s not
sales based incentive, it’s culture ­based incentive. Another thing that we do is we have this award
called, “Go the extra mile.” Now, a minute ago I said that you should do things for your customers,
you don’t have to be based around your product. This is called go the extra mile award, and this
award is given to the employee who does the most absurd, over the top, amazing act of service for a
customer, and let me tell you some of the stories that some of our people have done. And this is how
I’ve been able to grow a business to over a $100 million without spending any money on advertising,

ride to go somewhere. I’ve had guys changed car batteries. I’ve had guys clean people’s gyms, and

37:52 Speaker Cursing.

didn’t have any money coming, I was always like... I was impatient, like a lot of young people are,
and I was looking over here and over here and over here and trying to start this business and that
business and this business, and the reality of starting a business is it’s gonna take all your energy that
you have, so if you spread it out over multiple things it’s hard to make the one thing you want work. I
did that for a long time. And then the other thing I think I would do is, for the first 10 years in business,
the reason I didn’t make any money is ‘cause I wasn’t focused on customer results, I was focused on
me making money. And I think I would have learned the lesson upfront, like, “Hey look, I’m here to
provide this service for these people. Let’s focus on that, and it’ll take care of itself.” So that would be
something... That took me 10 years to learn. If you guys can learn that now, you’re 10 years out of the
hole, literally. Don’t worry about yourself. Worry about them. Don’t worry so much about selling. Worry
a lot more about helping.

05:41 DJ: So obviously, I didn’t wake up for quite some time the next day. [chuckle] And I had to

06:38 GC: Alright. Thank you. Thank you, man. And on that flight you mentioned something to me

07:16 DJ: Yeah, so it’s your reputation that precedes you when you walk into a room and when you

any place else. Listen, Shark Tank’s out now eight years, right? If you’re watching ABC, if you don’t

That is already set in stone and we don’t need to do the show any more. We do it for very specific

many people here watch Shark Tank with their kids? Raise your hand.
[applause]
08:06 DJ: So that’s absolutely amazing, that’s the main reason we do it. But we know that the glow of,

08:22 GC: Now, FUBU is the perfect jumping off point. I don’t know that everybody nows the story of

08:41 DJ: Right, yeah.

DAYMOND JOHN
08:42 GC: And so, what is the power of broke?
08:46 DJ: The power of broke is utilizing everything within your reach and we were raised with this
whole concept of you have to have money to make money. You have to know somebody. You have to
be educated and all that thing. And I didn’t have any of those. I didn’t have a famous last name either.
Well, I do have a famous last name, but if I tell Elton John I’m his son, he’s probably gonna not agree
for various different reasons, right?
09:10 GC: Yeah. [laughter]
09:12 DJ: The power of broke is to use your resources. And like my mentor, who will be here
Sunday night, Mr. Jay Abraham has taught me, absolutely, that the power of broke is using OPM, but
OPM shouldn’t be other people’s money. It should be other people’s manufacturing, other people’s
marketing, other people’s manpower, and other people’s mentors, and making money off of other
people’s mistakes. But this whole theory of needing money to make money is just bullshit, because
if you look at the top 1,000 wealthiest individuals in the world, 60% of them are self made men and
women. That means they were broke. And if you look at the people who win the lotto or over 60%
of professional athletes, they’re usually broke three years... Bankrupt three years after leaving the
league. That means that the tool of money was never of value and that means that if you think like
you’re broke and you activate the power of broke, then you’re going to be more equipped to win. You
gotta understand, the people who activate the power of broke more than anybody else are the people
who are wealthy. Those are the people that activate the power of broke more than everybody else. It’s
something that you have to do, and all that other crap that you...
10:31 GC: How do they activate it? Because I don’t know... I think a lot of people think, “Oh, if I had
money I would do that. If I had money I would do this... “
10:37 DJ: The number one reason why small businesses fail is over funding. It’s the fact that you go
out and you think you need a $100,000 or you think you need to take an investor in at an early stage.
If you’re doing $100,000 in business and you say to me, “Hey, I’ll sell you 50% of my company for
$100,000.” “Okay. Well, then what if your company get up to $2 million and you need more capital
infusion? I’m not giving you my 50%.” Now you’re gonna have to go raise more money, now you’re
down to 25%. Then, you gotta raise it again. Now, you’re down to 10%. Now, you’ve just lost the taste
for the business. Right? So, it’s not that... It’s using your things that are within your reach. See, real
entrepreneurs take affordable steps. People think that entrepreneurs are visionaries. They’re not.
Entrepreneurs are not visionaries, entrepreneurs do the best thing they can do today and then they
do it again tomorrow. And they fail often.
11:33 DJ: I’ll give you an example. Kodak was founded in 1888, they filed Chapter 11 in 2012. Now,
when they filed Chapter 11, over 80% of their employees were viewing their images on Facebook,
15% were viewing on Instagram, and a bunch of them had Shutterfly. Now, it’s not that Kodak didn’t
want to accept the fact that digital change was coming into the market. They had a digital platform
149

for 15 years that they were trying to perfect, but they would not give it up. They said, “This digital
platform is gonna make it, that’s it.” They couldn’t care about anybody else, so they stuck on that.
Now an entrepreneur who fails fast, here’s what he does or she does, within that same 15 years, that
entrepreneur would’ve started one business and they would’ve failed, because they would’ve learned
that they didn’t have all their trademarks and everything locked in in order. But then, when they
started the second business, they would’ve had their trademarks, they found out distribution wasn’t in
order. So when they failed, they start their third business, their third business they would’ve found out
that the funding needed to be properly in order. Then the fourth business... By the time they get down
to those 15 years, they become us, every overnight success takes 15 years. [chuckle] So that’s the
exact difference on entrepreneurs and... It is.
[applause]

13:46 GC: So what’s your advice to an inner city kid today? No money, no dad, no education, hates
school, or some of the people in this room, no connections, no money, no education, financially
illiterate, ‘cause they’re here.
14:03 DJ: Yeah. So for those who don’t know my story, my father and mother got divorced when I
was 10 years old, and my mother raised me by herself and I was an only child. I’m also dyslexic, I
didn’t realize that until maybe 15 years ago. I got left back in school one time and I never went to
college. So the three things that every entrepreneur needs we’re all given... Most of us are given by
God is three things, it’s very simple, it’s health, it’s desire, and it’s common sense. That’s it. That’s
really it. Health, desire, and common sense. And even without the health you can still try to figure that
out.
[chuckle]
14:44 DJ: ‘Cause there’s a lot of people that I know impress me, and they weren’t born as healthy
as all of us. So the key to success is to educate yourself every single day. Now I speak at a lot of
conferences, I speak at a lot of corporations, and we all do the woo woo and then go off and don’t do
anything after that, but we’re at a really dangerous time right now. We moved from the agricultural
age into the industrial age and industrial age into the technology age. And maybe if you were doing
something the same way you were doing it many years ago, the curve would’ve taken 15, 20 years,
but things are changing in one year, two years now. And if you were... My friend, Champ always
gives me this analogy, if you were a farmer and you were plowing the field at the agricultural age with
your donkey and you see somebody over there who’s now embracing the industrial age and they’re
plowing with a tractor. If you don’t learn how to operate that tractor, where to acquire that tractor, the
best way to get that tractor, if you say, “Don’t worry about it, I don’t need to do that, I’m gonna do this.”
Well, then there’s two jackasses on the field plowing that field, right?
16:08 DJ: And we’re at a very difficult time, and you never stop learning. Even ‘til today, some of you
are learning how to be a better husband or a wife, or how to eat better, or how to train better, or how
to do whatever the case is, you don’t wake up in the morning and know everything. And we’re at a
day and age where technology is changing the entire world, so you can either be a dinosaur or now
you can find ways to empower yourself from your laptop at home. I met a kid, he wanted me to help
him become a rapper. I saw him in a hotel lobby. I said, “I can’t help you become a rapper.” And he
said, “Okay, no problem.” We go out to the valet to bring our cars around and his car comes around,
it’s more expensive than mine. I said to him, “It doesn’t look like you’re doing half bad as a rapper.” He
says, “No, I’m a fine artist, I paint, but all my friends who paint with me, we all make
$50 or $100 a day painting so I have 100,000 people that follow me on Instagram and every single
Monday, I take a white shirt and I paint it on Instagram. I time lapse the painting and I tell people,
‘You can get this shirt for the next 24 hours and 24 hours alone. It costs $50 whether you buy one or
1,000, it’s up to you, but you’ll never see it again.’ Everybody that likes that shirt, prepays for the shirt,
I take their money over to the screen printer, I copy all the screens that were ordered and I mail them
out, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, and I do it all over again every single Monday.”
151

17:33 DJ: And he did $1.2 million in revenue. So think about that. He’s converting half of 1% of his
customers every single day, but unlike when I made a shirt for $10, I sold it to the store for $20, they
may have sold it for $40, I don’t know who purchased it or why they didn’t purchase it or what they
didn’t sell, they sent it back to me. He’s making it for $10, selling it to his customer for $50, he has the
analytics on his customer and he’s doing it all over again, but he’s not doing anything new.
18:03 DJ: He’s only doing what Publishers Clearing House used to do or Columbia Records used to
do, he’s just doing a new form of delivery. And if you don’t understand how to do this, you’re done. So
just like our parents told us or maybe you have to tell your parents, just like they used to tell us when
we went from high school or to college, something else that we wanted to find something out and they
said, “Take your ass to library and learn.” How many of you over 30 years old here know how to code,
know how to build your own sites, learned how to do pay per click, learned how to do all that thing,

You’re gonna go get somebody to build you a $20,000 website or you’re gonna roll up your sleeves

22:50 DJ: Well, first of all, something I’m absolutely obsessed about, I’m gonna do that. I’m gonna

You see, the good thing is today, with social media and things like that, don’t ever go to a focus group.

course, you give them away to your family members, at least your Aunt Betsy is gonna come back
and say, “Everybody loved the shirt.” ‘Cause she cares about you.
23:34 DJ: I live in New York. I stood on the corner of 125th Street in Harlem trying to sell shirts.
Here’s the best thing about standing on the 125th Street in Harlem selling shirts, they not only tell you
what they think about your t­-shirt, they tell you what they think about your mother, about what you’re
wearing, they tell you everything. So first get a proof of concept by real people. Then you mimic

DAYMOND JOHN
that in various different areas with various different ambassadors. Don’t go after the big people like

million of them are guys with lollipops and trench coats on. [laughter] But you look at 100 people
who equate to the same following who don’t have this public persona, 95% of the people love them

27:13 DJ: How do you get them in the system then? FUBU has a big 05 on all the shirts, all those

you need to first of all establish, “What is the partnership? Who are responsible for this and that and
what we’re gonna do? And what is the reward?” And if you can get that, that would be great. I forgot
to leave out, one of the other important things that I would get when I was first starting out if I was

28:11 DJ: That’s true. Besides obviously, my mother was my mentor but I didn’t realize that she knew

28:19 GC: Mom. Come on, Mom. [applause]
28:21 DJ: Absolutely. Absolutely. We never think our parents know anything. My daughter’s here
and she thinks I’m the dumbest person on the planet. And then the second mentor usually is what I

mentor. My third mentor was a guy named Tim who owned a grocery store in my neighborhood and
I used to go there and sweep up all the time, and it was interning, basically. And Tim would kind of
teach me the fundamentals of business at that time. And I always say this, if somebody’s running a
small business in your neighborhood for the last 20 years, they’ve been through recession, they’ve
been through gas hikes, they’ve been through everything. They know the fundamentals of business.
Maybe about seven years ago when Shark Tank really took off, I reached out to Jay Abraham, I didn’t

not.” About my kids, I never let a camera in my house. Oprah was gonna do a special on me she said,

38:28 DJ: It is. I’ll tell you why. A corporation looks at something and says, “What is our ROI?” Return

00:01 Speaker 1: Roddy Chong is the virtuosic violinist known for his multiple world tours with Shania
Twain, the best­selling female artist in country music history. Roddy was also the violinist for mega star
Celine Dion during her international tour and various television appearances. As the energetic rock
violinist for the Trans-­Siberian Orchestra lighting up 60 sold­out arena shows every winter for the last
decade, Roddy recently performed for an audience of two million people at the Brandenburg Gate in
Berlin. Roddy also performs with Kevin Costner & Modern West, with Costner claiming Roddy is a
difference maker on any stage.
00:40 Speaker 1: Through his virtuosity, Roddy has been able to interact with many of the top leaders
in various industries such as Howard Schultz, Kelly Ripa, Manny Pacquiao, and Tony Robbins. Roddy
has had the privilege of encouraging our Allied forces overseas and the US Armed Forces. He was
recently honored as a VIP rider with the US Navy Blue Angels, withstanding 7.5 G­forces in the cockpit
of an F/A­18 Hornet. When not on tour, Roddy serves as a corporate speaker, teaching individuals and
organizations on performing effectively, and was recently named by the Huffington Post one of the top
five business speakers to see in 2017. Roddy is constantly inspiring others, including his niece and
nephew who just happen to play the violin. Roddy exemplifies that when you dream big and perform
those dreams with massive action, the result will go beyond your imagination.
[music]
[applause]
04:14 Roddy Chong: Alright, thank you very much. My name is Roddy Chong and I’m a performing

[applause]
05:25 RC: Go ahead and drop those arms, roll the shoulders back three times, and have a seat.
[applause]
05:33 RC: Some of you are like, “That’s the best workout I’ve had all year.”

05:38 RC: We’re gonna use our voices next and before we do that, I have a vocal warm­-up. It’s the
letter A and it goes like this, “AAAA”. Okay, join with me, take a breath, here we go, and “AAAA”.
Excellent, from the good book ‘The 10X Rule’, to get us all on the same page, let’s read with some
gusto together, here we go, “People overestimate how well things will go and then underestimate
how much effort it will take to push things through. You cannot train or prepare for normal amounts of
resistance or market conditions. Prepare for massive action and you will win.” Can anyone relate to
this?
[applause]
06:34 RC: There have been times I’ve underestimated the effort and there have been times that
I’ve been able to surge 10X, and that’s what I would like to share with you. I came from a Chinese­
American household and in a Chinese­-American household you got two choices, piano or violin.
And the choice that was given to me was that violin and something that my parents would have me do
is get up in front of family members and old people and have to perform for them. So there’s a picture
of me with my clip-on tie and I’m smiling as if I enjoy the violin, right? Totally wrong, I did not like the
violin, this was just something that Asian people do and they make their kids play the violin. But I am
a little bit glad that I had this one talent but I’d be able to play violin and this is how the song would go
in a public performance. I would be trucking along in my song and then all of a sudden, bang. I would
hit a mistake, I knew it was a calamity and I would tell my violin teachers about this. And they would
say, “Oh, don’t worry about it. Nobody noticed.”
07:41 RC: Well, it didn’t help me at all because I totally noticed. It was in public, everyone noticed,
so I was all on my own with this. And with my little mind I’m like, “I’m gonna start over. Hopefully
no one noticed that.” And I’m going along with this song again and I could feel it coming, though,
sometimes in the exact same place, bang. I’d hit that mistake again and I knew even at a young age
that I was cementing the mistake. And I was not having a great performance, and it’s taken me a
couple decades of performing in front of live audiences, and getting the the right coaching, the right
coaching, to help me know that it’s not because no one noticed that you go through a mistake. You go
through a mistake because just beyond that mistake might be a custom­-made moment of brilliance
for you, right here.
[applause]
08:35 RC: We call this being fully alive and that includes mistakes, but you must play through them to
possibly get to that moment of brilliance. So I grew up with a band of guys, good guys, and
our ring leader was a football player named Todd [08:50]____Sitz, which was pretty cool. I was
more of a band orchestra kind of a guy and we were driving from Chicagoland up to the Wisconsin
Dells, so alright, someone’s from Wisconsin, excellent. Well, there’s these cliffs and then there’s
rivers underneath the cliffs, and we went to the top of this cliff and out of nowhere, Todd ran and
jumped off the cliff and everyone went, everyone just didn’t think. They just ran and jumped, and even
the dorkiest guy in my group ran and went. But I couldn’t move. I was paralyzed by fear and in that
230

RODDY CHONG
moment I had a very brainy, clever thought. Now brainy, clever thoughts are not good because I’m
totally in my head. I was coming up with an excuse.
09:33 RC: So I grabbed everyone’s camera and I said, “I’m gonna walk down the side of this and
take everyone’s pictures, so I could be useful.” So they’re having a good time running and jumping
while I was walking down the side of the cliff, and the problem was every step I took, I felt my dignity
shrinking. We went to the Dells. I went to my first year at Indiana University and whenever I ran up
to a challenge, I had this bad analogy in my mind that I wasn’t able to jump off this cliff. So our first
summer break, I told Todd, I said, “We gotta go back.” And we went back and we went to that same
area, and Todd said, “Do you want me to go first?” And I said “No, that’s what happened last time.
And I was totally frozen. I gotta go.” So, he made sure that the water levels are right so I’m not just
saying, “Go jump off of any cliff”, guys. But anyways, we have a picture of what happened and it’s
right here. We were so high that we left our shoes on, so we wouldn’t spank the bottom of our feet on
the water. But this is a picture of a boy becoming a man.
[applause]
10:40 RC: I did it over and over to make sure that I had really slayed that dragon, and 10X­ing isn’t

[applause]
10:57 RC: And by conquering this fear at a young age, I was given permission because I had

Three, two, one. Three, two, one. March it out, march it wide, and plant it like this. Punch to your right,

go. Right. To the front, right and left, double time, right, left, even faster. Come on, excellent, both

12:52 RC: We’re cooling down already, cooling down. Left arm over, right all the way over. Looking

13:42 RC: My parents were like, “Okay, you got a Bachelor’s degree and you’re running around
Chicago teaching aerobics. Okay.” I was driving in my dad’s hand­-me-­down Chevy Cavalier, had
about 200,000 miles on it. It was all rusted out and beat up. I hit the seat button on the radio and I
found a rock station, and there was a song. It had a violin in there and that song was called, “Flood”
by a band called Jars of Clay. Alright, some fans.
14:09 RC: And I had a friend named Becca. Becca had moved from Nashville to Lowville. I’m sorry,
reverse that. From Lowville to Nashville. And I called her up, and I said, “How’s Nashville going?” And
she said, “It’s going great, except, there’s this guy. He keeps chasing me around and asking me out.
I don’t wanna go out with him, and he has some claim to fame, that he produced some Jars of Clay
CD or something like that.” And I was like, “Becca, you have to go out with him!” And she did. And
so, she talked about me. And this guy liked her so much that she talked to him and he talked to her.
And then, he talked to Jars of Clay management. And the timing was very cool because Jars of Clay
was performing at the Double Door, a venue up in Chicago. And they took me out for coffee. And they
asked if I would join them on their tour. And I knew that this was one ­in a million shot. And I wanted to
take it.
15:03 RC: So I flew down to Nashville, and I knew that I had this little bit of talent with the violin. I
liked movement with music. And somehow, these guys liked it. And this tour went great. There was a
band called Matchbox Twenty that opened for us.
[applause]
15:18 RC: And it was crazy. But at the end of it I got a little disgruntled about money. So, to
document it, I wrote a letter and it went like this, “This is what was communicated to me, this is what’s
happening and this is what should happen.” And I showed my dad this three-part letter. And he said,
“Don’t send this letter.” And I was like, “Dad, this is based on principle.” Some of you heard that
before. And I sent the letter off anyway, multiple copies, and I got a FedEx letter back the next day,
that said, “Dear Roddy, we’re sorry feel the way that you do. Best of luck to you in the future.” That’s
right. I got fired. I wasn’t planning on that. And not only did I lose the only job I had, I lost that cool job
that I could tell my friends, “I’m on tour and I’m kinda cool.” And not only that, but I thought I’d messed
up the destiny that I had for my life. Because I knew that shot was one in a million and it put me into
a time of depression that went on for months. I was inconsolable. I wouldn’t talk much. And at night, I
would cry into the night. Sometimes I would cry out and just pray. And I was totally upset.
16:31 RC: But I knew that I could 10X, even in that situation and try. And I decided to drive down to
Nashville and I said, “I’m gonna shake hands with two people, and see what can happen.” And I was
driving on 465 past Indianapolis and typically, I like rock music. I like testosterone music. But this
232

RODDY CHONG
time, I came across a song. It was like a David Foster song, and it was a girl singing how she loved
someone and how that person was in love with someone else.
17:00 RC: And I had to admit, I liked the song. There was a violin in there. And I was humming along
to the song. And I actually started crying. And I started crying so hard that I started bawling. And it
was totally messy. And I had to keep the car on the road. And when the song crescendoed to an end,
I just said out loud, “What was that?” And I did not get an audible voice. But I did get what I called a
deep convicting thought that said, “Someday you’re gonna play that song with that girl that you just
heard on the radio.”
17:35 RC: And I didn’t have any direction. So I said that, over and over. And I would say things over
and over because I had other negative things in my head. Not only did you not, “You’re not gonna
make it. Your friends are making a lot more money than you. And you messed up the one shot that
you had.” I would say things over and over to try to give myself some operating room in my mind,
“Someday, I’m gonna play that song, with that girl, that I just heard on the radio.” And I went to a place
called Tower Records and I went inside and I tried to sing the song to the cash register guy. And it
went like this. “Oooo­oooo­oooo. Oooo­oooo­oooo.”
[vocalization]
[applause]
18:26 RC: Thank you. And the guy looked at me a little weird but he might have been a musician, it
was Nashville after all, and he said, “I think that’s Celine Dion.” And we went to a listening station and
we found the song and I bought that CD and I learned that song, it was called ‘To Love You More’.
I learned that song by ear, just in case. And I met some people in Nashville but nothing happened
as usual and I went back up to Chicago. Now I had a mentor, I had seen him performing with Kenny
Loggins, Steven Curtis Chapman, these artists, and I wanted a career like his. And he wasn’t really
my mentor by choice, somebody had his phone number and I would just cold call this guy and I would
ask him questions. And he would only let me get through one question, but I was always happy that
he would at least answer the phone. And something different happened where he called me and he
said, “Roddy, how’s your fiddle playing?” And I said, “Well, I don’t really like fiddle. I like rock and I
like violin, that’s what I do, rock violin.” And he goes, “Okay, well, I’m working with a country artist.”
And there was some artist he was working with, they’re looking for an audition and I just asked him,
“Country?” Do they know what I look like? [laughter] ‘Cause I just didn’t see the Chinese guy on CMT.”
[laughter]
19:43 RC: But I was still happy that Brent called me and so he said, “Well, how’s your singing voice?”
And I was like, “Oh, I can sing great, I can sing a high D.” I didn’t really know what I was saying but
I wanted to keep the conversation going. And he said, “Okay. Well, I’m working with an artist named
Shania Twain,” and that’s what I heard. So I typed that into the search, Shania Twain, and a website
came up that said the only artist in country music that was bigger than Shania Twain was Garth
233

Brooks. And even I’d heard of Garth Brooks so I realized this was not a small endeavor and I called
Brent back and I said, “I didn’t sound too excited last night, did I?” And he said, “No, you did not.”
“Well, I am excited. I can do this audition, they won’t have to look anywhere else, I can do this tour.”
And I ran and got the used CDs, ‘Woman and Me’ and ‘Come on Over’ and I learned these fiddle
parts by ear, just in case. Alright. I was getting prepared.
[applause]
20:45 RC: Eventually they flew me to Albany, New York and I remember being in that Holiday Inn,
they had a continental breakfast. And a continental breakfast for a musician is like getting paid,
‘cause it’s free food. I’m like, “ [chuckle] Hard boiled eggs with peanut butter,” I was going to town.
And I remember a truck came up in the front and this truck had mud on it and this guy came out, he
had overalls on with mud on his boots. I sized him up, he said his name was George and I thought
to myself, “That’s great, this is George and he’s just the driver and I need a ride.” So I’m walking with
him to his truck.
21:19 RC: Now my mom is the kind of person who sends me snail mail, mail with a stamp in the
mail. Does anyone have someone in their life that actually sends them stuff like this, Reader’s Digest,
stuff like that? Yeah. How to rent a car? How to not have your identity stolen? Well, that’s because
they love us so much. And my mom was always sending me, still does. And I sometimes read them
because they are actually pretty good. And the wisdom goes like this from my mom, “You have two
ears and one mouth. You really should listen twice as much as you speak.” I’m like, “Thanks, mom.”
Or, hang on a second, it might be how to have a good conversation, “Don’t talk all day long, ask
questions, you might learn something.” Or let’s not have people stand up, but let’s sit up in your chair.
Sit up in your chair, upright. Come to the edge of your chair and lean forward towards me as if this is
going to be the greatest nugget of wisdom for 2017.
22:25 RC: Let’s read this together, this quote from Abraham Lincoln, “Better to remain silent and be
thought a fool than to speak out and remove all doubt.” And I was like, “Okay.” So I realized that I
had expressed myself with a dumb letter and got myself fired and I’m sitting in this truck, I decided to
do the template that my mom had given me. I’m gonna ask questions, I’m not gonna talk too much.
And so I said, “What do you do?” And he said, “I help put tours together.” And in my mind I was like,
“Well, that’s interesting, ‘cause I thought you were the driver.” But I said to clarify, “Have you ever
done this before?” And he said, “Yes.” I was like, “Darn, he’s better at this than me. [laughter] I will
not get in the trap, I’m gonna ask a question. ‘Like what?’” And he said, “Well, I help put together
Bruce Springsteen’s E Street Band.” And I was like, “The E Street Band, I’ve heard of them and they
are rich.” [chuckle] And I realized this was not the driver, this was George the tour manager and my
audition had actually started back at that continental breakfast.
23:42 RC: And we drove up to St. Regis, New York and we went to Shania’s house and Shania
opened the door and gave George a big hug and they led me to a room and Brent Barcus, my
musician mentor was there and other musicians were there. And at the front of the room was a man I
234

RODDY CHONG
realized was the leader. His name was Mutt Lange. And Mutt said, “Do you have the song? Don’t be
stupid prepared.” And I was excited, that was the song I had worked on the most, had the fiddle solo
down and everything. And then he said, “Do you have the international version?” And I said, “I have
the version from the used CD store down the street.” And they had me listen to it and it was the same
song, but it was cut and pasted, my fiddle parts, enough to ruin the entire audition.
24:28 RC: And in that panic, the power went out, no sound, no light and I knew something was going
on that was helping me in this audition. And I just was grabbing a battery­operated jam­box and I
learned this song, the international version, and I mapped it out on a legal pad, I was very scrappy
and when the power came back on I stared at that map and I played it the way that they needed it to
be. After that, Shania said, “Are you ready to sing the song?” and someone told me about vocals but
they never told me about the song and I wasn’t ready and I had a little thought that I needed to ask
for five minutes, and I was intimidated because I was in the boss’s house, this woman, Shania Twain.
And I said to her, “Could I have five minutes?” Sometimes courage comes out in little ways. And then
she heard me and she called lunch.
25:20 RC: And so, I had an hour to figure out this song and I found a lifecycle and I was pedaling,
trying to get my body warmed up so I could get my voice warmed up. And when they came back from
lunch, Shania played the three chords and the melody she sang and I sang the harmony as best I
could and when we were done, she said, “You have a nice presence to your voice,” and it wasn’t so
much that a famous person was saying I had a nice voice, I just felt like I was moving along with my
destiny, that something was helping me outside of myself with this audition. And they told me that I
should go home and grab more clothes and come back and I stopped at home and got back as quick
as possible because I didn’t want them to change their mind. And I ended up doing two two­year
world tours with Shania Twain and I have a photo of yesteryear with this.
[applause]
26:15 RC: That’s me in my ‘Milli Vanilli’ hair, and the awesome thing about this photo is who would
have thought of a Chinese man in a country music band? It was beyond my imagination. One of our
first TV shows was Diva’s Life. And the divas were Aretha Franklin, Gloria Estefan, Mariah Carey,
Shania Twain and Celine Dion. And when I saw her, my jaw dropped and I wanted to tell her how
I was crying in my dad’s Chevy Cavalier and how much that song meant to me, but I had to hold it
together and play the Shania songs, which I did. After our performances, everyone ran to their trailers
out back but Celine is an extrovert, she was at the front of the stage signing autographs and I was
behind all these hundreds of people and I was trying to figure out with a lot of intention, how can I get
to her and tell her that I love this song so much?
27:10 RC: And in that moment, I had another one of these deep convicting thoughts and it said, “Go,
move, stage right.” And I didn’t think, I just went. And there was a guy, he was on the cellphone and
his back was turned to me and then he turned towards me and I recognized him, it was René Angélil,
Celine Dion’s husband and manager and I realized that this was the person I was actually supposed
235

to be talking to. And when he turned to me, I put out my hand and said, “That song, ‘To Love You
More’, I can play it anytime.” [laughter] No style, just go time. And Rene loved that clumsy energy and
he understood and he called Celine and the entourage over and he said, “Blah, blah, blah, blah” they
were talking French, “Violinist, blah, blah, blah.”
28:01 RC: And Celine is so gracious, she came up to me and said, “Nice to meet you, maybe we’ll
work together some day.” And then she floated off [laughter] and I was the happiest camper, nothing
had really happened but I was close. And I exchanged business cards with her booking agent and
that person called me and asked if I would put the song ‘To Love You More’ on video, they were
looking for a violinist for their tour and I was excited because I already had the video made and I
showed my dad, and my dad said, “Don’t send this video.” And I was like, “But dad, this is where I
think my destiny is, what I’m supposed to be doing and it happened in the car that you gave me.” And
he’s like, “Yeah, well, you also have this really cool job with Shania now and I just don’t wanna see
you looking unstable, not business­like, you just look like you’re gonna jump ship at any opportunity
and I don’t wanna see you lose both these jobs in one week.”
29:01 RC: And I remembered not listening to my dad’s wisdom and feeling the sting from before and
I decided just to follow my dad’s wisdom. I called that booking agent and I said, “I’m gonna stick with
Shania but I can play that song at anytime,” and they understood and I did the tours with Shania. And
two years into that first tour, we were going through Montreal and the manager asked if I could stay
the evening and if I could play the song to the music director that morning, something had happened
with their violinist. And I remember being in the Sheraton there in Montreal and I was jumping up and
down on the bed like a little kid because I knew what possibly could happen. I had been using that
song as a warm-­up piece for two years. I played the song for their music director and it was where it
needed to be and they asked if I would tour with Shania and Celine at the same time, and it was...
[applause]
29:58 RC: And it was out of the darkness and in the depression and I used 10X to just try to build
some character and crying out that led to something that was unimaginable like this. Because it was
not really industry standard, it was disruptive for both tours. Whenever there were concerts on the
same night, I would play with Shania and they would have a video of me with Celine. And I wasn’t
able to bring Celine to 10X Growth Con. But I have a video of one of our first performances when
I was very nervous but I was still playing note by note to get through and fulfill this thing that had
happened to me in my dad’s Chevy Cavalier. Would you like to see the video and have me play along
with it?
[applause]
30:39 RC: Let’s do it.

34:00 RC: Thank you. Thank you. I wanted to refer back to this diagram. Is there a mistake that you
have done? Is there a mistake that has been done to you? Let’s use the 10X principles and play
through that mistake. Just to have a chance. That’s all you’re wanting is a shot. Just to have a chance
to get to that moment of brilliance just waiting for you after that mistake. And to get from the moment
of brilliance... From the mistake to the moment of brilliance, this in­-between time can be very shaky
and uncomfortable. And I talk to myself like a little kid when I need to get from here to there and I say
things like this, “Let’s do this”, and I step into the situation whether that’s a sales call or an interview or
whatever you’re going through.
34:50 RC: I know that you are game. So let’s try this. It’s three words, “Let’s do this.” Here we go,
on three. One, two. “Let’s do this.” And in the performing arts we say nothing happened. [chuckle]
That’s a level one. So let’s take it to a level three. Let’s stand up, stand about shoulder width apart.
Get yourself grounded and use whatever blade, right or left, you need to cut through the air to get to
where you’re going. Three words, “Let’s do this” on the count of three. One, two, “Let’s do this.” In the
performing arts we’d say that was level three. Something happened. But where we’re going is 10X.
It’s outlandish. It’s unreasonable. Never seen before. Beyond our imagination. Let’s take it to 10X
and shake the walls. Here we go. One, two, “Let’s do this.” The person on your right might need to
be encouraged. They look good but they’re scared to do something. So let’s encourage that person.
Level 10. Here we go. One, two, “Let’s do this.” The person on your left might need to be shaken,
might need to be extended out of themselves. Last chance, last chance. Level 10, 10X, here we go.
One, two, “Let’s do this.”
36:14 RC: Hands up in the air. 10% more, we can always give 10% more. Slowly have your arms
come down. And this time, if you’re near someone, grab a shoulder. And if you don’t know that

We’re not just talking, we’re doing stuff. And we’re gonna do our vocal warm-­up. Here we go, join me.
“AAAA.” Louder. “AAAA.” Here we go, let’s read from the great book. Be obsessed or be average.

00:19 CK: Okay, a tough act to follow, thanks, Roddy. How amazing is Roddy Chong?
[applause]
00:27 CK: Unbelievable. Well, I am so incredibly excited to be here. I’m also very humbled to be
