[applause]
00:58 CK: How many Gen X’ers we got in the room?
[applause]
01:03 CK: How many baby boomers we got in the room? [chuckle] How many Gen Z’ers do we got in
the room? I met a 17­year­old yesterday. He bought my program. You are a Gen Z’er, raise your hand if
you’re in here. I think his name was Josh, somewhere.
[chuckle]
01:23 CK: Well, again, I am so excited to be here. We have a lot of content to cover, really exciting
content, especially because we have 45 minutes here. And I wanna leave you guys with tons of
nuggets that you could ultimately implement as soon as you are done with this convention. As soon
as you leave this room, I want you to go, “I could do this, this, and this.” And I want you to do it.
01:46 CK: Let me tell you a little bit about my beginnings, where it all began. I actually grew up
in South Florida, Delray Beach. I went to Spanish River high school, any sharks in the audience?
[laughter] I went to Spanish River high school, I was in Television and Production program and my
teacher Mr. Waddell was a huge influence and not only in my life but my career. Who was here for
Damon John last night?
[applause]
02:15 CK: He gave a shoutout to teachers and as soon as he said that I said, “Wow Mr. Waddell was
such a force in my world, I’m not sure if I was gonna have the means or the power or the know­how to
create that radio talk show treatment which ultimately was the catalyst to my career. At 16­years­old I
was always that young girl that loved the View and the Today’s Show and Good Morning America and
241

Katie Couric and Oprah and Diane Sawyer. These were all my mentors, my role models I worshipped
them. Then one day they all started talking super negatively about the next generation. These
teenagers, everything was so negative. Would you say that you’ve heard more negative things about
this generation than positive? Raise your hand. I hate that, who else hates that?
[applause]
03:11 CK: Thank you. So instead of sitting back and just being a 16­year­old trying to get into college,
I talked to my mom who is in the room today, some of you might have met her [03:23][chuckle] And
I said, “Mom, we gotta do something.” Only programming for millennials for teenagers at the time,
this was 2007 was Hanna Montana and ‘16 and Pregnant’. Not my favorite programs, didn’t really
empower us, didn’t elevate us, didn’t motivate us, didn’t inspire us. We were the next generation,
we’re the first generation to grow up with technology. We’re digital natives, we needed a platform
to come together to voice our opinion to understand one another, to learn from experts all over the
world. Think about 2007, there was no such thing as millennials, the word millennial. There was no
such thing as influencers. YouTube was for funny cat videos, Facebook you needed a college EDU
and My Space was not the space you wanted to be in.
04:16 CK: So I took a yellow spiral notepad, mom and I went for a bagel and tuna fish and I wrote
down topic after topic after topic of what I wanted to discuss amongst my peers and this generation. I
created a radio talk show treatment and I walked in to Clear Channel Studios in Palm Beach, Florida,
and I had a pitch meeting with the president of the station. I walk into the room and the podium is the
end of the table and I’m the other end of the table and he told me to sit here and he was sitting over
there. Kind of intimidating, 16­years­old my first rodeo, I’m sitting as proper as possible. I gave him
my whole pitch, I gave him my whole spiel, thought I was really doing a good job. Best poker face
I’ve ever seen. He looked at me like I was an alien, then without saying a word, got out of his chair
and left the room. What do you do at that point? Do I stay, do I leave, did I insult him, am I crazy, did
I waste his time? Five minutes later, 10 minutes later, 15 minutes later my butt was still in the chair.
I was still in the room, I meant business. He came back into the room 15 minutes later and had a
contract and said, “I wanna help you start your career.” And that was the beginning to it all.
[applause]
05:49 CK: Let that be a message for a moment. 16­years­old, I met 17­year­olds yesterday, 18, 19, 21,
22, 23, it is so exciting that we have so much young entrepreneurship, young hustlers in this room
because there is so much opportunity for us to take advantage of. It doesn’t matter how old you
are, we all have the same opportunity to establish our influence. Growing online community. Have
multiple ways to monetize, further establish our brand authority. All of that opens doors for so many
opportunities.
06:26 CK: Around the same time, I signed the agreement contract, the very first radio show was
scheduled for March in 2008. I announced to my friends what I was doing with Teen Talk Live.
242

CHELSEA KROST
How excited I was for them to be a part of the show. My four best friends looked at me and they said,
“Who do you think you are? What are you gonna talk about? What are you gonna do? Who’s gonna
listen? Who cares.” And they stopped talking to me. At that moment, I contemplated giving up. I
contemplating letting the bullies win. I contemplated letting the negative affect the positive that I really
felt about this platform, that I felt about this generation, that I felt about this change that needed to
happen. And if I let the bullies win, I don’t know if I’d be here right now.
07:26 CK: So for all of you who are currently in a state of mind, “Do I? Don’t I? I was told it wasn’t a
good idea. Market research says it’s not smart. The investor shot me down.” It’s always a yes until it’s
a no. And if it’s a no, you make it a yes and you try again. And that’s what we have the ability to do.
Thanks to social media. Thanks to online marketing. Thanks to internet marketing, we can try and fail
and try again. Refine, repeat, consistency and it will eventually start to work. We just have to do it the
right way and take the time to learn how to do it.
08:09 CK: You could see this beautiful woman next to me in the picture? I believe that there’s always
moments in our career that are a catalyst moment. For example, the catalyst moment was Teen Talk
Live. So radio talk show host, one of the youngest in the country. However, I wasn’t
done. I knew I wanted tad more to do. This woman, Kristine [08:31] also known as my
African Angel, walked into my life and changed my life forever. Not only that she changed my life
forever, she changed my career forever. I’m getting emotional. Kristine, unfortunately lived in Rwanda
and witnessed her father get beheaded in front of her. Her and her seven siblings went running
through the jungle for the next three days until they found shelter. Ten years later, a wonderful family
friend of ours happened to meet Kristine doing mission work in Africa and sponsored her to come to
the United States. I got to meet Kristine, and Kristine asked me if it was okay for her to come on Teen
Talk Live and share her story of survival. And I said, “Yes!
Absolutely!” She came on to the show. She shared her story of survival and then she shared
something that I totally wasn’t expecting. She said, “Chelsea, you and your listeners need to know
how lucky you are to live in America.”
09:39 CK: So I immediately thought the things that you’re probably all thinking. Food, water, shelter,
opportunity, education. She said, “Because you have feminine hygiene care products.” As a radio
talk show host and as a podcaster, the last thing you want is dead air. There was some dead air after
she said that. Feminine hygiene products? She goes, “Chelsea, what people don’t understand is
that women in Africa don’t have the means or don’t have accessibility to these feminine hygiene care
products that allow them to leave their home, go to school. They end up missing too much school,
they fall behind, they can’t graduate, they don’t have a degree, they don’t have a diploma. In Africa,
if you do not have a degree or diploma, you cannot get a job.” Feminine hygiene care product had
this ripple effect for women in Africa. I turned to my mom again and I said, “We need to do something
about this.”
10:43 CK: I wouldn’t be here if it wasn’t for the inner courage that I had, but if it wasn’t for the support
system around me, you’re only going to be as successful as the people you surround yourself with.
243

And a lot of people are gonna try to tear you down in this process. And you need to be very picky and
choosy of who you let in your space. My family happens to be my ride or die tribe. I’m so excited that
my entire family is here with me today.
[applause]
11:15 CK: My dad, my mom, my husband, my brother, his girlfriend. Everybody supports me,
everybody played a role in my career. We ended up putting a mission trip to Africa that summer. My
brother was the camera man. My dad was the producer. My mom planned the entire trip and all of the
components that went along with it, and I was the host. My husband did not come to Africa at the time
but he has been my cheerleader since we were 13­years­old.

scale content for my clients as well. My business partner and I were not the right match and that’s

know your business partner. Make sure you have the right values, make sure you have the same

dog food. I didn’t do all the basic elements of running a business, I didn’t build a sustainable pipeline,

see them, you wish you never did. The RCMP arrested that company with fraud and embezzlement,
and they owed me $35,000, and I was an unsecured vendor. That might as well have been
$100,000,000 to me. I was dead. But the problem... I was getting on a plane to Liberia, Costa Rica for
our wedding. So I say, “Honey, there might be some challenges when we get home. We’re gonna be
in trouble, don’t worry.”

stick the key in the door. It’s cold, it’s raining and I just start to cry. I start to cry because I

called, “Why you? Why you now?”
06:50 Speaker 1: It’s very simple. Grant is mentioned in the Wall Street Journal, he says that in the

JAMIE SHANKS
you. The why you now, is it turns out we sell a time and attendance solution that allows you to track

because it was awesome. It looked great. I put money and attention into it and I spent all this energy

for me is key in terms of my branding on my photos. I’m constantly looking to push the boundaries
of how to create better images, better branding on all my social media channels, on my website.
Everything I do is branded and it all connects together.

0:01:00 TG: Taking athletes and making them unstoppable.

0:01:06 TG: Well, we’ve been fortunate to work with some of the greatest athletes out there. Michael
Jordan, Charles Barkley, Hakeem Olajuwon, Scottie Pippen, Kobe Bryant, Dwyane Wade and
hundreds of others. Attack Athletics is always evolving. We have old school work ethic with new
school principles and innovation. In the three categories that I divide the individuals up, we have
coolers, we have closers, we have cleaners. The cooler’s an individual who’s good at what they do.
The closer’s an individual who’s highly motivated, who if you give them the script, they’re going to get
you that desired result every single time as long as a lot of variables aren’t thrown at them.
Then we have the ultimate person that I call a cleaner. A cleaner is unstoppable. They’re gonna get
you that end result no matter what’s thrown at them, what variables come their way, what questions,
what answers. No matter what’s thrown at them, they are going to figure out a way to get that end
result.

0:02:13 TG: And this applies to every job, every individual that’s out there. We have teachers that are

principles apply to anybody and everybody that’s out there no matter what they’re trying to achieve.

0:09:00 TG: Over and over again. So here are your definitions. Who in here is a cleaner? If you have

0:09:38 TG: Nowadays, we raise a society for getting rewards for just participating. Who has kids

0:10:37 TG: Let’s look at it. Coolers. Coolers can have a great day. Everybody said, “I’ve had a great

Michael Jordan said, “He practiced so hard, that the games became easy.” You don’t make your sale,

0:13:40 TG: Cleaners. They don’t wanna congratulate their success. They just wanna keep going.

0:16:29 TG: All this, again, comes from a mindset. It comes from a mindset. You’re not born with this.

0:17:10 Speaker I got the drive to make it happen.

0:17:17 Speaker 45 years.
0:17:18 TG: 45 years. Guess what? It’s got a expiration date. Everybody in here. It has an expiration
date. Now, you can have more than one “it” moment, but it does have an expiration date. He says

is. Unless there’s an action behind it, it doesn’t mean anything. I know so many people that are
passionate about things. I know so many people that have inner drive but unless you put some action

0:18:17 TG: Don’t come outta here and say you did not get what you expected out of this situation

say, “Oh, I gotta hang around positive people. That’s another excuse. “I’ve gotta be around positive

my face to make me feel good.” That’s exactly what that means.
0:19:02 TG: How many of you go to happy hour? Who goes to happy hour. So no one’s been to a

the most miserable people hanging around at happy hour. They’re telling you how they don’t like their
relationships, how they don’t like their job, they’re not making any money. Who knows those people?
Who knows those people? And if you don’t know those people, you are that person. You are that
person. Nothing is going to change unless you change your mindset. That’s the first thing you have to

TIM GROVER
0:19:49 TG: It’s my little cheat sheet over here. I got one over here. It’s not a sprint, it’s a marathon.

12.0 flat. Get on the treadmill, run two miles, not 26, run two. Let me know if that’s a sprint or not.
That’s another excuse, time is not on everybody’s side. So you gotta stop lying to yourself. Tomorrow
is not promised. You get as much as you can done today ‘cause tomorrow is about something else
and the next day is about something else. Two things happen, either you’re getting better or you’re

0:21:14 TG: Now, when we talk about in the book, I have 13 different characteristics of what I call a
cleaner. These are the traits that these individuals, the most competitive people, have inside of them.
This isn’t about being a good person. I’m not telling you this. This isn’t about winning a popularity
contest. This is about a mindset of the champions that are able to do it over and over again. Whether
it’s in business, whether this in life, whether it’s in sports. It’s about being able to repeat the top over
the top repeatedly. Here we go.
0:21:56 TG: Notice that they’re all labeled number one. The reason I labeled them all number one is
because when you label something one, two, three, four people think number two is less important
than one and so forth down the list. Everything is as equally important. Your second kid isn’t less
important than your first child. Your second spouse is more important than your first spouse but not
your kids. So everything is labeled number one. The other thing I have here, that really gets to me...
People say I got a plan A, I got a plan B, I got a plan C, I got a plan D. Every single one of your plan
should be a plan A. ‘Cause if you’re selling me plan B, you’re telling me plan B isn’t as good as plan A.
Everything is a plan A. You just have to find the right person for that plan to fit into.
0:23:05 TG: Now, let’s take these traits that we have one at a time. You go harder when everyone’s
had enough. Everyone talks about man I work hard. I work hard. Working hard is not enough. I
know a lot of individuals that are talented, that work hard, that never succeed at anything. You gotta
work hard and you gotta work smart but the key to working hard, you have to trade one addiction
for another. Failure is an addiction. Success is an addiction. But what happens is when you trade
failure for success. Guess what? Now you gotta trade another addiction. You gotta trade hanging out
with your friends for loneliness. You gotta trade and sleep for not sleeping. You gotta trade for being
normal for being crazy. I’ve not met one successful person that isn’t a little off. They have to be off.
[applause]
0:24:24 TG: If you get complimented and say you’re crazy take that as a compliment because they
can’t think the way you can, they can’t work the way you can, they don’t have the vision that you
have. So when I go train my clients and they need a referral from one referral to the other. So when
I decided to finish working with Michael Jordan, Kobe Bryant was having issues with this knee,

so Kobe calls MJ and says, “Hey, man I’m about to hang this up.” And he goes, “Well, what’s the

using him anymore.” So Kobe thinks about it he goes, “Well, tell me about him?” He goes, “Listen,
Grover knows his shit inside and out, but he is the biggest asshole you will ever meet.”
0:25:24 TG: Now, there’s a big difference between a asshole and the asshole, okay. I’m the asshole.
When it comes to my business, when it comes to my business, and I’m working with my clients I’m
like that thorn in that rose. Leave me the fuck alone. Let me do what I have to do because if I mess
up, my client messes up. He’s not performing well, she’s not performing well, I’m not performing well.
If I’m gonna make the mistakes, let me make the mistakes. Now, I’ve had the chance to meet a lot
of you individuals out there. I’ve taken pictures with you. I’ve been nice, I’ve been [0:26:17]____, I’ve
done everything. That’s who I am when I’m not working. I am the rose. I’m in a different color rose and
so forth. But when the clients come in, when Grant’s doing his stuff, when Brad’s doing his stuff, when
Victor’s doing his stuff, they are the thorn and that’s the only way they know how to do it. That’s the
only way they know how to do it. This is not about a popularity contest. It’s about going harder when
everybody else has had enough. You see the people that come in. You know what the cooler does?
The cooler shows up to work on time, he leaves on time. You wanna see the most successful people?
Walk outside after work. Go out at 5 o’clock you see a bunch of people leaving work. Go out at 6:00,
you’ll see less people working. Go out at 7:00 you’ll see less. Each hour you’ll see less and less.
0:27:17 TG: And those are the individuals that you will see climbing the ladder. They’re the ones that
they have the offices in the corner. And that’s a decision you make. That’s a decision you decide.

difference between here and LA. When you’re not doing business here, it’s a new day in China. You
can pick up the phone, you can still do what you need to do. If you decide not to make that decision
that’s on you. You go into the zone and you control the uncontrollable. Everybody in here’s got
emotions. Everybody. Emotions make you weak. Plain and simple. Emotions make you weak.
0:28:05 TG: The greatest individuals, business, sport they play with one emotion. One, that’s it. That

give me your fucking throw and I’m gonna step on this motherfucker.
[applause]
0:29:00 TG: ‘Cause you know why? They don’t give a shit what anybody in this room thinks. Because

They don’t play for you. They don’t play for anybody. They play for themselves. That’s what they do
because they know if they win, they control the situation, everybody’s gonna win. When Cardone
302

TIM GROVER
University wins, everybody wins. When Life Speed wins, everybody wins.
[applause]
0:29:34 TG: Here’s one I love. You know exactly who you are. We got a lot of issues with that in here.
Because society tells you to be something different. You know what? You talk to please. You talk to please
others. What happens when you’re young? You’re running around, they called it the terrible twos, you’re
all over the place, you’re on the back of the school bus, you’re doing stuff, you’re tearing things up. “What
are you? Sit down. Don’t do this. Don’t do that. Don’t do this.” You kill your instincts. You kill your instincts.
You know what relentless did? It gave you the permission to be who you are. Even when he wasn’t selling
one. He was going through his demon stages when he was fighting, he still knew who he was. You have
to know exactly who you are and don’t let people change that about you.
0:30:34 TG: If you are a nice person, stay a nice person. If you are a dickhead, be the dickhead.
Know exactly who you are. That’s what makes you successful. That’s what allows you to take your
business, yourself, your mindset to that new level. Stop trying to worry about what everybody else
says. You go your whole life, your whole life, trying to fit into this small group. You try to find the right
group of friends, you try to find the right sorority or the right fraternity to be in, you try to find all the
right click to be in. And who the people we idolize the most? The ones that stand alone.
Those are the ones who we’re trying to be with. Those are ones we idolize the most. So why are you
spending all that damn time and energy trying to fit into a group. You know what? Have them fit into
your group. That’s what you want. People we were talking about earlier they were talking about, “Oh,
a bucket list.” What is the bucket list? These are the things I wanna do. You know what my bucket
list is? My bucket list is for all of you people in here to say I wanted to meet Tim Grover; that was the
bucket list. That’s the bucket list.
[applause]
0:32:01 TG: I’m gonna talk about this one here. I see people already crawling into their seats a little

individual’s got one. You spend so much time suppressing it and not learning how to use it and control
it. You’ll never gonna be able to reach that top level unless you know what to do with your dark side.
So, let’s talk about how this kind of works a little bit.
0:32:44 TG: So let’s go about... Everybody’s got superheroes, all the movies are going on right now.
They’ve been going on for years. Let’s take... I may get these names confused, if I do, work with me

0:33:03 Speaker Superman.
0:33:04 TG: Bruce Wayne’s alter ego is?
303

0:33:08 Speaker Batman.
0:33:11 TG: Bruce Banner?
0:33:13 Speaker The Hulk.
0:33:14 TG: Hulk. Okay. When I ask you who the alter egos were, you guys said that Clark Kent’s

Bruce Wayne is the alter ego. David Banner is the alter ego. So a lot of people here when they go
to work, we have 2,500 people in this room. I guarantee it. Out of the 2,500, out of the 2,500 in this
room, more than 2,400 of you, when you go to work, you put the mask on.
[applause]
0:34:27 TG: Hold on, before you start clapping, let me finish. Let me finish. You think Michael Jordan

home and put on the mask. That’s what they do because they cannot turn it off. They cannot turn it
off. Because the need to finish is so freaking strong, you guys just don’t understand. This isn’t what I
said early. It’s not about being a good person. It’s not about being a good person.
0:35:20 TG: Those individuals, they don’t go to work, they become the work. If you go to work, that
means you got to put the mask on. If you become the work, you are who you are. And that’s what the
dark side is about. It’s about being the most ultimate competitor to get what you want over and over
again. We all have a dark side. You don’t believe me? It’s about your ego. It’s about your secrets.
It’s about your desires. It’s about things you don’t want anybody to know about you. That’s your dark
side. You can either use it, learn to control it, it will take you places that you’ve never been before. But
if you don’t know how to control it, it’ll destroy you. And that’s why so many people are afraid to tap into it.
Why? So many people are afraid to tap into it. You’re not intimidated by pressure. You thrive on it.
0:36:31 TG: Grant, 10X, who created the noise? You did. He created the noise. And guess what you
do when you create the noise. He gets to control the fucking volume. He gets to decide how high he
wants to turn it up. He gets to decide how low he wants to turn it. It’s up to him. Let me tell you what
pressure is. If you’re ever put into a pressure situation, it’s a privilege. It’s a privilege because not
everybody has the skillset or the mindset to be able to put in a pressure situation. That means the
boss believes in you. That means somebody believes in you. That means your skillset is so sharp that
you can handle it. People break under pressure but also pressure creates the greatest things that you
want. It creates diamonds. It creates roads. It creates automobiles. It creates individuals. So you can
take pressure and take it either way. It can either destroy you or it can take you to an unbelievable,
unstoppable level. But in order to be able to thrive under pressure your knowledge about your
product, your confidence in yourself, the ability to adjust, to have that mental set, is gotta be so
304

TIM GROVER
superior. So if you get a chance to be under pressure, it is a freakin’ privilege ‘cause not everybody
gets to be in that chance.
0:38:10 TG: You see it all the time. Coaches are drawing up plays or doing all this. It’s one of the
Lakers games, Phil Jackson, he’s sitting there, he’s drawing up this play, “Kobe, I want you to do this.

ball and get the fuck out the way. Because if I’m gonna win this game or I’m gonna lose this game,

pressure. When everyone hits, in case of the emergency, button, they’re all looking at you. Who saw

0:39:07 Grant Cardone: Yes.
0:39:07 TG: No, he doesn’t. You know why? See if he carries the chip on his shoulder, somebody can

Nobody can touch it but yourself. That way it can’t get knocked down. The only way you tap into it is
you have the ability to connect with the dark side and then you control that chip inside of you. Don’t
give anybody else that control. How many people hear this all the time? “Man they really know how
to push my buttons.” or “You got to learn how to push employees’ buttons.” A great leader does not
push buttons. A great leader empowers the individuals. He pulls the individuals’ buttons. So when
they need to push them, they push them themselves. That’s what they do. If you give the ability to
somebody to push your buttons, that means you’ve given them 100% control over how you gonna
act, how you gonna speak, what you gonna do. Never give up that situation. Never ever.
0:40:15 TG: In case of an emergency, be that person. Be that person. Be the one where everybody’s

handled the situation. Had the medical staff and he was already prepared for this. He goes, “Hey,
nobody is dying on my damn watch. It ain’t happening.” You don’t anticipate these things but you plan
for them ahead of time. And this is his first event.
[applause]
0:41:02 TG: You don’t compete with anyone. You find your opponent’s weakness and you attack.

Everybody knows how to compete. There’s maybe 10 people in here that know how to win. That
know how to win over and over again. Everybody can compete at something but the ability to actually
win, to find the opponent’s weakness and to attack it and to go after it and continue to go after it.
Everybody’s got one. Everybody’s got one. Everybody’s got a weakness. Everybody’s got a disability.
305

Find it. Find your opponent’s weakness. Find their disability. Attack it. Go after it. This is business. If
you don’t go after it, they’re gonna go after you. I guarantee it.
0:42:04 TG: The same person that’s patting you on the back, that’s shaking your hand right now, in

You see, you’re either getting attacked, or you’re gonna do the attacking. You find their weakness,

good people. I’m talking about being able to win, and compete, and what the ultimate competitors can
do over and over again.
0:42:46 TG: You make decisions, not suggestions. Every minute, every hour, you’re sitting around,

suggestions. Everyone loves to play a armchair quarterback. They love to sit in here, and say, “Oh,
man, I watched this on TV. Why didn’t he throw over here, why didn’t he do this, why didn’t he do
that?”
0:43:49 TG: An NFL quarterback has about three seconds from when he hikes the ball, and he has
3,000 pounds of individuals coming at him at full speed. He has about three seconds to look out on
the field, observe everything that’s going on, and make the right play. Three seconds, that’s it. In

they’re going around the curve? Anybody take a guess?
0:44:38 Speaker Inch. Half an inch.
0:44:42 TG: Pretty close. Three inches. Three inches. That’s how sharp these skillsets are. That’s
how good these individuals are. That’s how decision-making happens. You hear this all the time
in sports, “Don’t think, don’t think.” Roddy was up here, playing that violin. Is he still there, by any
chance? Alright, listen.
[applause]
0:45:18 TG: Chicago boy. Let me ask him. When you were up here playing, what were you thinking

0:45:26 GC: Nothing.

TIM GROVER
0:45:27 TG: Nothing. How many years of training did you have to do not to be able to think about
nothing?
0:45:35 GC: Decades.
0:45:36 TG: Decades. Decades. So when they tell you, “Don’t think,” don’t think doesn’t mean,
you just wing it. It takes years and years of education, research, understanding, trials and errors, to

cannot see the basket when they’re shooting. If you notice, when he was playing the violin, everybody
was listening to the beautiful music that he was playing. I was looking at it more from an athletic
standpoint. He never once looked at his fingers. He never once looked at the violin. He looked at the
audience. He was looking upstairs because it was all about instincts. It was all about instincts.
0:46:35 TG: When they say, “Hey, trust your instincts.” But when you’re a cleaner, and I am gonna

and continue to be the best, over and over again. He didn’t let anything stop you. Does he have

individual. Alright, we may be taller, we may be shorter, skin color’s different. The difference is not on
the outside, the difference is on the inside. That’s where the difference is.
0:47:39 TG: How about this one? You don’t have to love the work. But you gotta crave the end result.
There is not a single person in here that loves 100% of everything that they do. There’s not one
person in here. But you gotta crave that end result so much, that the work is irrelevant. You have to
do the work, you have to do the process. Everyone talks about the process all the time. The reason
I don’t like to talk about the process because you have to do the process. It’s not an option. But
you know what, the end result is an option. The end result is always an option. Why do I have Bill
Belichick out there? You know what he said after he won the Super Bowl? Had his little trophy, had
his little championship thing in the locker room. This is exactly what he said. He said, “This is great,
but I’m five weeks behind next season.” This is done. This is done. I’m over here now. I’m five weeks
behind next season. He’s not thinking about what he just accomplished. That’s done. That’s over with.
0:49:00 TG: Grant’s not thinking about his numbers for last week, last month, “How can I do better?
I’m not satisfied. I crave that end result so much. I gotta keep going, I gotta keep going.” That’s the
mindset. Each individual in here, if you have the skill set, you have to develop the mindset. That’s
what relentless teaches you. That’s what it’s about. It’s about having that mindset, “I know I’m going a
little bit over but I’m gonna keep going. I’m the last person here.”
[applause]
307

0:49:43 TG: You rather be feared than like. We spend too much time in life trying to be light, we

one of these programs.” That should instill fear into your competition. You know what? They have an
advantage. They have an advantage. Michael Jordan told me, he goes, “I don’t pay you to train me.”
He goes, “I pay you not to train anybody else.”
[applause]
0:51:00 TG: First six years with him, I was not allowed to work with another professional athlete,
nobody else. “I don’t pay you to train me, I pay you not to train anybody else.” If you have a resource,
you can expose it and use it, take it. Everyone talks about sacrifice, “I have to sacrifice.” There’s a
difference between what you’re willing to sacrifice and what you’re actually going to sacrifice. People
talk about it all the time. We have all these diet books out here. We have all these self-help books out
here. We have all these exercise books out here. Guess what? Every diet book you read, oh it works,
it works, but no one’s lost a freakin’ pound by just reading a diet book, you gotta put it into action.
0:52:08 TG: No one in here is going to be successful from just buying this stuff. A lot of you
individual’s gonna buy all this stuff, you’re gonna seat here and you’re not gonna know what to
do with it. You got all this stuff here and you’re gonna be like, “Alright, what do I do next?” Skillset
versus mindset. If you have the mindset and you have the skill set, that’s when you become a feared
individual. Every person in here, we’re not looking for friends, you’re looking for allies. Allies are the
people that would tell you what you’re doing right, what you are doing wrong.
0:52:50 TG: The quickest way to end a conversation, if you do something wrong, “I fucked up.” That’s
it. Guess what, now when you admit that, you gotta go fix it. You gotta go fix it. There’s no come back
for, “I fucked up.” End of conversation. But now, do you have the ability to fix it? You trust very few
people and they better not let you down. The more wealth you get, the more successful you get the
less individuals you trust. The less individuals you trust because they have their agendas. Dwyane,
when Lebron came over to the Miami Heat, there was this big struggle the first year, about who is the
team leader, who is doing this. They didn’t win the championship.
0:54:02 TG: Dwyane would not go of the reins until he knew he could trust Lebron 100%, in order
for him to get that end result. Many times, he wasn’t even sure about that. But when he was finally
had that trust factor in this, he said, “Boy, don’t let me down. Don’t let me down.” So when you have
individuals and you delegate them some responsibility, you give them some work, they better not let
you down ‘cause they’re not gonna get that opportunity again. When I deal with my athletes, I try to
eliminate every single variable detail there is possible to find out why they are not performing at their
optimal level. I’m talking about what pillow they slept on, what bed, what they had to eat, what kind of
socks they wore, what kind of shoes, that, they’re getting on argument with their wife? What’s going
on? I need to know it all because somewhere in the story there’s a reason you are not performing at
your level. That’s my job. The little things matter. They matter so much.
308

TIM GROVER
0:55:28 TG: And it’s usually the little things that people skip over that let you down. If somebody
ever tells you, “It’s okay.” It’s never just okay. It’s never just okay. If that’s all you’re gonna settle for,

than one way to get what you want. Everybody has limitations. We talked about this earlier. But if you
surround yourself with the right team, who are able to see those failures coming, and allow you to
make adjustments, not everything is going to work. It is not going to work.
0:56:32 TG: No one starts at unstoppable. Nobody in this room starts at unstoppable. You know what

make failures, you make adjustments. So what determines if an individual, when they say, “They’re
born with it. This person has it.” There’s something that’s happened in every individual’s life in this
room, including mine. And I guarantee that it’s happened to everybody in here already. Except, I’m

0:57:28 TG: How you dealt with that circumstance is gonna determine whether you still have it,
whether you can fight through failure, whether you can harness your dark side, or you’re destined to
be a cooler. Plain and simple. Did you run from it? Did you face it? You’re gonna fall down. I’ve fallen
so often. The one thing you see when people fall down, they fall down, they brush themselves off. I
don’t brush myself off. You know why? That’s 30 seconds I could be figuring out what the hell I need
to do. I’ll take the suit off and get another one. I don’t have that time. You gotta keep moving. You
don’t recognize failure. Grant’s told you how many stories about how things haven’t worked. “I can’t
do this event. It’s not gonna happen.” It’s happened. Oh, boy, did this happen?
[applause]
0:58:51 TG: You don’t celebrate your achievements. You always want more. When this is over
with tomorrow, you know what he’s gonna say? You know how he says, “Great.” He’s gonna say,

here. We never see you there.” You know what my answer to them was? “I never see you guys at the
fucking bank.”
[applause]
0:59:49 TG: I’m not saying don’t recognize what you’ve accomplished. Sit back. Enjoy it for a

309

1:00:21 TG: What the hell are you celebrating? Now, I’ve been hanging out with a lot of people,

1:01:13 TG: If you can’t handle your liquor, don’t drink. Plain and simple. It’s an option. Number two,

[applause]

1:03:27 TG: I’m gonna give you a little secret, what works even better? Just say hello. That’s it,

1:04:13 TG: He’s a sales master. He’s mastered the sales industry. He’s teaching people how to do

1:05:23 TG: If you have the ability that you know you can loose everything today, and within the

1:08:15 TG: Come here. Come here. Come here. Come here, everybody. Come here. Now, listen, I

1:08:25 GC: Oh, shit.
1:08:25 TG: Hold on, hold on, hold on. I know it’s his show, but right now it’s my stage. Every other

[applause]
1:08:42 TG: Alright. Give me your notes. [chuckle]
1:08:48 GC: So, look, my wife... I’m so glad you’re here, man, because my wife constantly, “You’re
not nice to people. You don’t say it nice. There are so many different ways to say it nice. You’re hard
on people. Why are you so hard on everybody?” And I’m like, “I’m not... “ Can you talk about that?
1:09:06 TG: You are who you are. Here’s the thing. If you started to be nice, they’d be like, “Are you

1:10:13 TG: Here’s your question. Is there a cost to it? Yes, there is a cost to it. There’s all these

This was when a parent could let you walk to the store without getting arrested for negligence.
[laughter]
0:02:00 GC: And we’re walking to the store, man, and... Dude I’m proud. I’m so proud, I’m so proud,

And I’m walking to the store and I’m playing with my quarter, and the quarter comes out of my pocket

and you freaked out? Now you really appreciate it. “It’s gone, oh, my God!” Okay, I realized that day
how short my arms where ‘cause I couldn’t reach it. I started crying ‘cause I lost my quarter. I went
home and told my papa, “I lost my quarter, I lost my quarter.” And my dad said, “Never play with
money, never play with money, never play with money.” Oh, okay. My grandfather grabbed me later
and said, “Never go anywhere with one quarter.”

said, “You have to have courage to eliminate your family. If they’re not contributing to the asset, if

0:19:21 EC: I don’t wait for motivation. I go off of discipline because it takes discipline to gut up and

I’m the behind the scenes chick. Sherry and I, we’re the sniper force. You know what I mean? I’m like

off.” We’re a good team and that girl has my back. Purple Lamborghini, I listen to that song, going

does anyone love the gym?” But I get it because it makes you strong and you feel so good and it
builds confidence. Running on the beach, setting targets and achieving them, that’s self-confidence.

goddammit, I’m gonna do it.

0:22:37 EC: Thank you. Well, guess what? We think you can. We think you should and this is why:
The definition of an empire is an extensive operation or sphere of activity controlled by one person or

0:23:11 EC: It doesn’t say it was started by one person. An empire is never started with one person.

my husband says, “You’re gonna speak at the Growth Con in front of 2,000 people,” I say, “Okay.
I’ll do whatever it takes. You want me to dig a ditch? I’m digging a ditch. You’re gonna pay for my

323

0:24:44 EC: Everyone has an empire, including Sabrina, my seven year old. Everyone in my

0:25:47 EC: But I ask you to take a look at what does your empire look like? Is it expanding?

oriented or problems? Greatness or gossip? What does your empire look like? Have you ever even
thought about it? I didn’t actually think about building an empire until... I mean, it wasn’t even an
empire... I’m gonna jump, I’m jumping ahead, but I didn’t think about creating a life that I actually
wanted until I was in Los Angeles. It was the third loser I was on dating, you know, those idiots. Sorry.
0:26:49 EC: But this one was. It really wasn’t even the guys’ fault that I picked these idiots. It was my
fault, because I had always thought guys hold me back in life and they hold me down, and I never
wanted to be married. I never thought I wanted to have kids. I wanted to just be like the single chick
that ruled the world. I wanted to be a big movie star, and that’s where I thought my life was gonna go.
And so, I pulled in these guys that made me right, that all guys hold me down and I picked the worst
of the worst. I’m talking going to jail, I’m talking drugs, cheating, like the biggest drama I could find
and that’s the one I went with to make myself right about that. But then, I decided, “You know what?
I’m done with that game. I’m done with the insanity of that. Who am I really? And what do I wanna
do? And who is that guy?”
0:27:52 EC: My friend, Judith... Some chicks are weird. I’ve never really fit in with a whole bunch of
chicks, believe it or not. They’re just so bizarre. I was telling her this, and she’s like, “You know what
we need to do?” And I’m like, “What?” “You need to make a list and write everything you want on the
list and we’re gonna twirl it up and you’re gonna put it in a copper locket thing, a tube, and you’re
gonna wear it. And then, you’re gonna pull them in.” I’m like, “Oh, what? Okay.”
0:28:24 EC: I’m writing the list. I didn’t understand it, but I wrote this list. I wrote over a 100 things
down on this list in the tiniest print I’ve ever written. I went 6’2”, green eyes, ethically ambitious, no
hair on his butt or back, but he has hair on his head. I was really specific here. She told me to be
specific. But I got, really, more deep than that. I went, “Has good relationship with his mother and his
sisters, likes animals, is ethically ambitious.” I remember that one stuck out to me. “Doesn’t do drugs,
doesn’t do alcohol, is monogamist.” I was like, “Wow, those are awesome.” So I twirled it up and I
324

ELENA CARDONE
wore the thing until it turned my entire neck green and fell off. But I saved the tube and I looked at the
list and I went, “You know, I wrote this list and I’m like, I expect that guy to just show up in my life.”
But I really looked and I’m like, “But that guy wouldn’t pick me right now,” I’m gonna choke, because I
wasn’t good enough for that guy, at that time, because I knew what I was doing with my life and who
I was. I wasn’t strong, I wasn’t powerful enough to have him. So, I really went to work on myself. I
stopped drinking for three years, I didn’t take a drop of alcohol. I was like, “You know what? I’m done.”
My whole life, I was never an alcoholic.
[applause]
0:30:00 EC: I was never an alcoholic. I was a recreational party girl. I grew up in New Orleans, so I
can... I know how to shoot a shot of whiskey.
[laughter]
0:30:13 EC: But I was recreational drug using. I quit everything, and I was like, “Who was that? Who

commercial. And Grant knew the director of the commercial and he shows up on set, and then he
gets my number from the director and he calls me. And I’m like, “People don’t do that in LA. You’re not
supposed to give out somebody’s number when you work on a set. That’s so inappropriate.” And he
calls me and he has this conversation with me and he says, “Hey, my name is Grant Cardone.” I’m
like, “Ugh! Another one of those Hollywood guys.” And he was like, “When people hang out with me,
their life tends to get better. So I want you to get a pen and a paper.”
[applause]

0:33:43 EC: So, we’re married and 2008 happens. We get hit with a lawsuit for $60 million from one

willing to look at the signs ahead of time. The signs were all there. The red flags were there. We

up with somebody. So we have, 2008, a $60 million lawsuit, a million dollars just going out to the
lawyers like it is nothing. I never experienced anything like this. I have a baby on the way, the Lehman

0:35:13 EC: And I’m like, “What? What?” He says, “We’re going to die.” And I said, “Excuse me? Does

[chuckle]
0:36:26 EC: Two hours goes by and I’m like, “Shoot, is he dying in there?” [chuckle]
0:36:32 EC: “Maybe I should bring him some water? No, I have to be tough.” Three hours goes by,

ELENA CARDONE
and he walks out the door, and he puts a manuscript in my hand. It kills me. I’m so sorry. He puts a

[applause]
0:37:00 EC: “I figured it out.” [applause]
0:37:23 EC: He said, “Don’t get confused. People don’t get rich off of books. The book’s not gonna

betrayed women.” But I let that go because I found the one that I trust and I wanted to build an

0:42:05 EC: And there’s always a person, I always find a person. Whether it’s as silly as the

0:44:13 EC: It’s so incredible to me. So I don’t care what I look like, or who I offend. Most of the

331

0:56:37 EC: Anyway, whose included and whose excluded? That’s one of the most difficult ones. I
used to let everyone in. Everyone could come in, do whatever they wanted inside the palace gates.

Whose is worthy to be in your group? Who are you? Are you gonna be worthy enough to have a
good group? Are you willing to get there? It’s a question you have to ask yourselves. Who makes
the decisions? When you know your roles in the group or in the empire, you don’t cross each other’s
paths. You don’t have this power struggle. Grant and I aren’t having power struggles. I know what
decisions I get to be boss of. He knows which decisions he gets to be boss of. Who executes the

were to go to them with a problem with Grant, they would be like, “What? Are you crazy? Go freaking

1:04:51 GC: When we said, “Hey, the dogs have to go.” Now, the back story of this, I was doing a

[laughter]
1:05:41 GC: “You never kill the dog on TV, man. People love their dogs.” So talk about the dog story.
1:05:50 EC: Well, again, when I had the two kids, the dogs were no longer an asset. Even the

you walked into the... It loved me, loved Grant too. If you walked into the house and you were my
friend, that bird would be like “Ah! [1:06:11]
.” And people were terrified of this bird. It was
like Satan, reincarnated, this bird. But I said, “There’s no way I’m bringing a kid around that bird.”
So, I got rid of the bird, then the two kids come, and the dogs are no longer an asset. They became
a liability to us, in a couple of different ways, one got a little aggressive. They needed to be walked,
they needed attention. I just couldn’t physically do it anymore. It just became a liability. And I looked at
334

ELENA CARDONE
Grant and I said, “Either we are the coldest, craziest people in the world or we need to do what’s best
for us right now and get rid of the dogs.” So we found 100 acres, Jerrod found, actually, a 100­acre
farm where the dogs got sent off with these Arabian horses and they had chickens and...
1:07:10 SC: That was after.
1:07:12 EC: That was after. Yes, that’s right.
1:07:13 GC: Yeah, yeah.
1:07:14 EC: So, we sent the dogs off. And Grant and I didn’t... It was just so easy, it was such an
easy decision.
1:07:19 GC: We made a decision on one afternoon, the next day, the dogs were gone. There was no
emotional... We didn’t cry about the dogs being gone because this is when the purpose has got to be
bigger than the little emotional stuff that you’re getting caught up in everyday.
[applause]
1:07:33 EC: And what are you willing to give up?
1:07:34 GC: What are you willing to give up? What are you willing to give up? What are you willing to
give up? We were in Los Angeles when they raised taxes. I moved from Houston to California. I went
from a 0% to a 10%. I was fine, I’m like, “Okay, it’s fair.” I don’t mind doing the deal, 10%, California
didn’t do anything to contribute, but I’m like, “It’s a great place, it’s beautiful, dadada... “ But when
they started going to 13%, and then doing this, and I’m like, “We can’t keep doing this.” 2010 had hit,
I told Elena, I said, “You’ve been here 25 years, I’ve been here 25 years, all our friends are here, our
beautiful house is here, we’re set here, it’s time to leave here.”
[applause]
1:08:13 GC: You have to give up, man. You gotta give something up. Last question is: What have
you given up in this marriage in order to get the other stuff? You keep smiling, [1:08:23] .
1:08:23 EC: It’s interesting that you ask that, because I think the pat answer would be I gave up a
career or 95 episodes of a show when I had bought a house and had cars and a career or... But the
truth is, I honestly gave nothing up. Because I had to actually sever that cord in order to create what
I truly wanted, which was a family, which was kids, which was a man that I trust and love that could
help so many. And I can help so many by doing what I do. And it wasn’t like I had to give anything up,
it was just I had to be strong enough and courageous enough to cut a cord and go in another path
from the one that I originally envisioned. He’s not 6’2, doesn’t have green eyes, and it doesn’t matter.
It’s unbelievable to me.

0:30:39 HN: So when I first... The first real job that I had in TV, I was a researcher at King World,
Inside Edition, American Journal, Rolonda had a daytime talk show. And King World was horrible,

overtime with the Kings, they paid you half time. The fuck is that?
[laughter]
0:31:08 HN: That’s how the industry works, they tell you, that’s entertainment. So I heard a rumor
they were looking to hire a researcher for a pilot. So I quit my pretty cool full­-time job to go to a four­,
six­week job. I’m like, “I’m out of here. I’m doing this thing.” I interviewed for that job and it’s a pilot for
a show and I walk in and they’re like, “This show is not gonna go. This is vanity project. The star has
a big contract with ABC Disney, this ain’t nothin’.” That show was “The View.” They handed Barbara
Walters a pilot to appease her, ‘cause she was getting angry and uppity or whatever and they were
like, “Okay, let’s let her shoot a pilot.” We paid everyone to show up, paid Lou Diamond Philips, who
was in “The King and I,” paid him $2,000 to be a guest on a pilot, ‘cause he’s like, “No one’s gonna
see me, I’m not doing the pilot.” Had to pay the celebrities to show up.
0:32:06 HN: No one thought that show was gonna go. No one was doing a round table, no one was
doing that. So as the researcher, so when we came up with the idea for hot topics, I’m like, “Well,
that’s me.” Somebody’s gotta research. Ain’t nobody else in the house. I’m the only one here. So I
ended up producing the hot topics of the show for the first two years of the show and it taught me so
much that when I hand you a bunch of these ideas, I wanna unpack them and if you guys can...
Well, see me later if you have questions. This is just what I know. If it doesn’t land, you don’t
understand it, help me help you. I’ll explain it the best I can. So it was my job to get in to 5:00 AM
and research the topics of the day that go through the news, scroll through everything and figure
what topics that the girls could discuss at the table. The first days, weeks of the thing, I would walk
upstairs, hair and makeup and I’d be like, “Okay. Here’s... “ I’d hand ‘em all the sheets. And everyone
like, “We don’t like... We don’t like any of these.” [chuckle] Every day for like, a week. I was like, “Are
we all workin’ for the same show?”
0:33:21 HN: I would say, “Okay, I got you.” We’re talking [0:33:23]____motherfucker now. You

produce her. Star Jones, I gotta produce her. Joy Behar, I gotta produce her. I have to figure out what

0:33:55 HN: For real, for real. I tried to interview her for Star Power and she’s like... Oh man, she

00:49 JG: Okay. A good portion of you. I really just wanna start by telling you a little bit about myself

Y’all in that boat where you grew up kinda in the middle? And we’ve heard a lot of stories about
people that have had to overcome some serious adversity. Bobby Castro, 15 kids, homeless, there’s

people and I hear their stories, I’m like, “Man, they must have some incredible fire.” And I had to kinda
find my fire because growing up in the middle, everything’s about comfort. And everything is about
just enough, everything is about more, I guess, planning your day for tomorrow rather than really

02:01 JG: And looking five years and 10 years down the road rather than just looking at next month

was a problem for me was what to do with that work and how to channel that to go do something
really, really big. My dad taught me a lot of things. But Grant taught me a lot of the things that my
dad couldn’t teach me. And my dad was a sales guy, he worked in retail and I had the opportunity
to be around him and go into the dealership with him, he worked in the motorcycle business. And I
got exposed to transactional sales at a really young age, you get to talk to a lot of people and get
involved in negotiating transactions. I guess at a young age, it was kind of a natural thing for me, and
I know that sales people are created, they’re not born.
03:18 JG: But I was born and I was really extroverted. I was the kid that everybody was always like,

based success. I knew nothing about selling. It was a disaster for me, they’d be talking about steps to
a sale and I’d be losing count in my pocket trying to count on my fingers. It was a mess for me, but I
365

had a good attitude. I had personality­based success. Raise your hand if you’ve ever had personality­

04:07 JG: You just get what you get because you got a good personality. Man, I had no idea, I had no

05:16 JG: And so what happened was I had everybody around me telling me, “You’re 20 years old,

06:04 JG: And so, I got stuck for a while, and I remember that there was a point in my life where I

And then I’m broke. And then I’m on unemployment, and I’m like, “hat happened?

07:04 JG: So, over a period of about a year and a half, I cleaned my act up and decided that I really

what’s wrong?” So I kept calling, I kept calling. So for a month and the half, every single day, I called

you in front of Grant today for an interview.” I was like, “Okay, great, what time?” He goes, “Noon.”
10:53 JG: I was living in San Diego, if you all don’t know anything about the commute between San

I came to work for Grant, it was 2009. I didn’t know any of this was going on.
That Grant had just been put in this situation because of the financial crisis and he was forced to

11:40 JG: I had no idea that I was coming into a hornet’s nest, because I’ll tell you, there ain’t nobody

and he needed to expand, and he needed to not be singularly focused on one thing, and he needed

him at the helm from 2009, we launched a virtual training platform, it’s called Cardone University, y’all
have probably heard about it. We virtually had zero clients when I started. Today we have over 2,000.
And I’m telling you this for a specific reason. We have 60 employees in the company, we have over
100,000 active students in our sales platform, and we’ve delivered over 26 million lessons in a period
of about seven years. Go ahead, give it up, man, give it up.
[applause]
12:48 JG: I’m telling you this, this is important because this was not Grant’s business. Grant’s
business was seminars and real estate, but he got threatened and he realized that he had to eat his

And so maybe you guys are at a point right now where your business is changing and you need to
reinvent yourself. There’s a lot of things happening in business and the economy, the elections and
politics, there’s a lot of change happening right now. And so maybe you’re in that point where you

different perspectives when you’re looking for the right vehicle ‘cause that’s what Grant was for me,
he was the right vehicle. He was the right vehicle, it was the right guy, the right time, I got in and I
went for a ride.
13:45 JG: Okay, but I didn’t show up for the trip without luggage. I brought everything with me, I

that’s the leader. When I came in, I researched Grant, I found out what he was into, I found out what
he was about, and then I met the guy and I’m like, “Dude, this guy has some drive. This guy has
some purpose, this guy has some fire, this guy’s showing up at the office every day.” I’m like, “I can
get behind this guy because of the amount of push that he has.” And so what I did, it was, I decided I
was willing to do whatever it takes to get on that rocket ship ride, calling every single day for a month
and a half over and over and over again, no return phone calls, knew everybody in the office because
I knew that I had found the right vehicle.
14:48 JG: I’m not so sure he would have hired me today if the business was where it was at, but

at, “Hey, what can I do to become magnetic? What can I do to actually start drawing people into me?”
15:38 JG: And I’m gonna share some things with you today that we do in our company that create
368

JARROD GLANDT
that magnetic pull into our company where we attract people. We’ve got 18 sales people in our office
and there ain’t but two of them that are from Florida. Every single other person moved from out of
state on their own dime. Most of them from across country to come work for us because of what
we’ve been able to create, a magnetic culture where people just... They need to be involved in what
we’re doing. So I’m gonna share three things with you today. There’s a lot of things that we do in
our office that are unique. There’s a lot of things that we do in our office that contribute to how much
success that we have and how much we get out of our team, but I wanna share with you three things
that really stand out more than anything else.
16:31 JG: Number one, action over planning. Before I talk about my first day on the job, I just wanna
tell you that there has been a lot of action over planning going on just even today. Just make the
decision and then figure it out. My first day on the job, I get hired, I’m answering phone calls for
Grant... Answering phones, trying to make some phone calls, trying to make a list. Grant walks into
the office... How he walks... Walks into the office and he looks over at me... Huh?
[background conversation]
17:17 JG: What’s up, man? He strolls into the office and you just feel this woosh, and I look over and
I see him standing at me and he’s like, “What’s your name?” I said, “My name’s Jarrod Glandt.” He
said, “What are you doing?” I said, “I’m making a list of people to call.” He said, “Boy, I’m not paying
you to build a list. Pick up the phone and make a call.” And I was like, “Oh my God, what did I get
into? This is gonna be tough. Pick up the phone and make the call.” I knew from that day moving on
that things were gonna operate a little differently in the company.
17:58 JG: Because what we do is we don’t get caught up in details of planning, we make a decision
and we move on it. And that doesn’t mean we have to know everything that happens along the way.
We have to have clarity on what we’re trying to get to, and then we just start making progress every
single day. A lot of people get bogged down in mechanics and details, and it ends up preventing them
from moving forward. The 10X Growth Con, we decided that we were gonna do this the third week of
December. When we announced it, we didn’t have a venue, we didn’t have speakers, we didn’t have
a date. We just said, “After all these years, we’re gonna do an event.” And we were thinking we were
gonna get 500 people here.
[applause]
18:48 JG: I think I need to hear what 2,000 people sound like. [applause]
19:00 JG: So, what we did is we said, “Hey look, we’re gonna do an event. We want it to be good.
We want it to be the best. We wanna bring great speakers. I don’t know when it’s gonna be, but let’s
start promoting it.” The day we decided that we were gonna do an event, before we had anything
else figured out, we’re promoting it. That’s how we roll. We do not add time to decisions. So, what
ends up happening is you have to decide to live with the consequences of either taking action or not
369

taking action. There are consequences either way. Sometimes consequences can mean a little bit
of confusion, a little bit of chaos, a little bit of unorganization. Sometimes it can mean tough phone
calls or hard problems, but the consequence of no action is everybody else in this room right now is
somewhere else other than right here today. Go ahead, give it up, give it up.
[applause]
20:08 JG: So what we’ve made the decision to do in our business, because Grant lives like this, is he
says, “I’m willing to live with the consequences of action every single time over the consequences of
no action. Not even an option.” The second thing, targets. Everything has a target. Book sales, phone
calls, demos, turn calls, everything that we do has a target. We come up with the Growth Con, the
first action we take was the target. We don’t talk about how we’re gonna market it, how we’re gonna
advertise it, who we’re gonna try to get to the conference, where it’s gonna be. We don’t do anything
else.
20:57 JG: The next thing we do is we say, “What is the target?” And then we push on those targets
hard. No matter what, never ever, ever do we ever lower the target. We actually increase the
responsibility to make sure that we hit the target. So when we set a target for live streams or we set a
target for ticket sales, and we realize that the clock is running out, it isn’t, “Hey, Jarrod, you think we
got a little overambitious on the target?” That’s not the conversation I have with Grant. I sit down in
his office, he goes, “You need to figure this out. This is your responsibility to hit that target.” He puts it
on me, and then I gotta go on to the team and, “Guys, this is our responsibility to hit this target.” And
we do this all the time, like if something’s not working for us, we’re constantly reworking things. We
change our website every day. We change our strategy to market to somebody every day.
21:58 JG: We’ve changed our strategy today just to put on the event multiple times throughout
the day, because we’re nimble, because we’re always trying to hit the target, and we push, and we
insist, and we inspect that the target gets hit. Clear communication across entire staff, we have staff
meetings every morning just so we can cover our targets, and that requires that we have somebody
that’s leading from the front and really driving everything towards the target. You have to be present.
I was talking to one of the fellows that’s here, and he was talking about culture, he’s like, “How do I
get this 10X thing in my company?” I was like, “Man, you gotta be present.” Grant has no way to get
10X out of us if he ain’t ever there. He’s relying on me. I ain’t Grant Cardone. And then you gotta be
ruthless with the targets. He likes that word, ruthless.
23:00 JG: You gotta be ruthless with them, you have to make sure that people know that there are
targets that need to be hit and there are consequences for not hitting them. “Rewards and penalties,
rewards and penalties, rewards and penalties.” I’ve heard that over and over and over in my head,
“Rewards and penalties.” ‘Cause we gotta hit the target, more than anything else, we need to hit the
target, and if we’re not gonna hit the target, we’re gonna do everything possible so that the team
knows that we never bail out on the target. So when you’re saying, “Hey, am I in the right vehicle?” If
you don’t have these first two things, you’re not. You’re not in the right vehicle. And if you’re in your
370

JARROD GLANDT
company and you’re figuring out, “Hey, how do I bring more people?” It’s because you’re not giving
them a clear target or a clear focus, or the push, or you’re not reacting faster than your business is.
23:53 JG: The third thing, assume responsibility. We have high levels of responsibility in our office.
It can be a little humorous actually because whenever something goes wrong, everybody wants to
accept responsibility for the problem, “It was my fault.” “No, no, it was my fault, my bad. I’ll do better
next time, my fault, my fault.” Good problem to have. This right here, assuming responsibility, is what
got me to move from San Diego to Los Angeles where I didn’t know anybody to work for a guy who I
didn’t know, making less money than I was making before, and slept on an air mattress for almost a
year. How many y’all slept on a air mattress before?
[applause]
24:34 JG: Love me that AeroBed. But it’s got an automatic pump. [laughter] Responsibility is
important because you can’t control the things that you refuse to accept responsibility for. If you can’t
accept responsibility for it, you become the effect. You become the effect of something else, and we
just refuse for that to happen in our business. We refuse to be the effect of something happening to
us. The business gets torn up in 2008, 2009, people aren’t going to seminars, they’re not sending
their people to workshops, they don’t have money for travel. Assume responsibility for the situation,
change directions, move the business online. Promotion on XYZ new product launch isn’t working,
assume responsibility and re­direct.
25:33 JG: We had a situation with a product that we launched, and we went through a social
shutdown. There was quite a bit of chaos in the office. Google was threatening to basically pull us
off of all their AdWords, Facebook was saying, “We will close your account down,” because of some
wording. It said, “Millionaire.” They’re really protective of the word millionaire, I don’t know if you guys
know that or not. Because there’s a lot of scam artists out there, people that don’t have good products
and they take advantage of people. There’s this panic in the web team, “Oh my God, we’re not gonna
be able to advertise on Google anymore. Argh, they’re gonna shut down our Facebook page. What
are we gonna do?” And Grant walks in and he says, “We don’t need any of ‘em. I’m gonna accept
responsibility for the situation and we’re gonna figure out a way around this.” And we did.
26:27 Speaker [26:27]____cleaner!
[laughter]
26:28 JG: He’s the cleaner, that’s right. He is the cleaner, I can say that. So the transformation of the

business, for our team, that we apply on a daily basis. I want to go over a few more, only practices.
These are things that we implement in our business on a daily basis to make sure that the 10X thing
continues to flow and perpetuate in the organization. We have a two­ring rule. If you’ve ever called
371

the office and it rings more than two times, it’s a problem. If Grant hears the phone ringing... He’s got
this thing, in his office, away from the sales floor, away from customer service, he can hear the phone
ringing. And he will get up, “Pick up the phone.” Because we want people to know that we’re in a
hurry to help ‘em.
27:34 JG: “Thank you for calling Grant Cardone’s office, what can I get you information on?”
Everybody answers the phone the same way. We never rely on one of anything. One social platform,
one phone call, one text message, one video, one customer, one industry. We seek multiple flows in
everything that we do so that we go wide, because a wide structure is more stable than something
that’s narrowly focused. Commit first, figure it out later. This was something that I really struggled
with because I got caught up in the details a lot, and Grant, being around him has given me the
opportunity to see this in action. And for me, it was commit to the opportunity before you figure out
where you’re gonna live.
28:29 JG: It was commit to hanging out with different people before I knew who I was gonna hang out

in the organization to make sure that we have a sales culture in our business every day. Cashier, or a
receptionist, accounting, customer service, personal assistant. Literally anybody in our company can
earn commission for selling products. So you want to say, “How do you create a sales culture?” Dude,
reward people for the things that they produce. The guy answering our phones right now came from
Starbucks. Grant said, “Hey man, this guy makes my coffee perfect every day, he never whines, he
never bitches, he makes it perfect every time and he’s always got this huge smile. There’s gotta be a

it”. If you can’t afford to get the skills you need right now, you’ll never gonna be able to get it. And so
it will make such a difference for you. What makes you tick? Not what brings you joy, what makes
you tick? What makes you want to win? You wanna have a strong team? A strong team is designed
by people who want to win, not people who want to work. People talk about working all day long.
Working is nine to five. Winning is entrepreneurship. That’s the difference. So you have got to know
what makes you tick. I love the race. I love to be a thoroughbred. I love you to tell me, “I can’t.” Can
you imagine the number of people who came up to me and said, “You gonna do Mary Kay and you’ve
got a Harvard business school degree?”. “Yes, I’m gonna do my life, you do your life. You can’t give
me any extra money anyway.” When I get home, and I get into my big pink Escalade. And everybody
in the neighborhood knows exactly where I am. And I stop with the light and they go like this, I like
“Mm­hmm. Mm­hmm. Mm­hmm”
[applause]

at three in the morning. I’ve been up all night trying to get a flight to get to my father.” So that’s in the
middle of this amazing conference, a life interruption hits somebody. I want you to hear me. In life
there will be life interruptions. There will be things that come to your life that you would have never
ordered from life’s diner. Nobody orders lupus, cancer like Les Brown is fighting. Nobody orders a
divorce from life’s diner. Pay attention.

back. But while you’re feeling the sting of your setback, life is preparing your comeback. Somebody is

18:15 TS: Every day I’m hustlin’. Every day I’m hustlin’. Mr. Anderson, the white guy. I said, “Mr.
Anderson, my name is Timmy Storey, I’m a very good basketball player, you could ask my coach. And
I come from a good family and you need a new dishwasher because I’m noticing the dishes are all
dirty.” The owner of his own restaurant he goes, “Man, they are all dirty.” He said, “Well, how old are
you?” I says, “I’m 15 and a half and I know you can’t work til you’re 16.” And he says, “It’s good to be
16, aren’t you?” He says, “You’re hired, Timmy Storey.” I started as a dishwasher for Mr. Anderson.
Every day I’m hustlin’. Then he put me to waiter. Every day I’m hustlin’. Every day I’m hustlin’. I don’t
miss work. I don’t miss work. I never miss work, for real I don’t. Every day... ‘Cause I didn’t like being
po’. I don’t like the smell of being po’. I don’t like being looked down upon. I like owning who I am and
rising up to the place that life intended me to be. Somebody clap your hands and shout.

19:50 TS: You gotta... Look at me. You gotta fight. I was at a wedding in Laguna Beach and the bride
was two hours late ‘cause she didn’t like her hair so she left to get it re­did. Magic Johnson and I were
walking around and Johnson goes, “Number one, can you imagine she’s two hours late?” And we
were talking and he says, “You know what’s amazing?” He said, “People expect people like us to cave
in and cower because we’ve been through difficult times. They don’t understand what it’s taken us
just to get where we are.” Here’s what I wanna say to you. You have come too far to be regular. You
have come too far just to be okay. You have come too far just to be almost. Look at the hell you’ve

[laughter]
22:53 TS: In two weeks I’ll be speaking again in South Africa. I go into the townships and I speak to
broken kids. I go to the schools and I speak to kids that have been broken and you should see their
spirits rise up as I talk. Now watch, watch, watch, watch. And as I was leaving one of the townships,
a little girl who is 11 she says, “Tim Storey, hold on just for a minute.” I said, “What?” She says,
“Are you just telling us we’re gonna be great to make us feel better or do you really believe it?” Can
you imagine that? I said, “Who are your best friends? Get your best friends and get your teacher.” I
said, “I’m gonna have a one on one session with you.” All these little 10, 11­year­old people with their
teacher started talking to me about their dreams. Like, “What do you wanna be?” “A ballerina.” “What
do you wanna be?” “I wanna be the president.” “What do you wanna be?” “A lawyer.” Not one said, “I
wanna be bankrupt seven times.” Because when you’re little you think utmost thoughts. Say this. Say,
“I have utmost thoughts.” Clap your hands if you really do.
[applause]
24:12 TS: Come on people! [applause]

27:42 TS: It’s about 12:08 at night. Quincy Jones, he likes to say up late. So I say, “Quincy, I got
a question for you. How come some guys... “ Listen to this, it’s good. “How come some guys, they
have one hit and then that’s it?” And he gets up. He goes, “Oh man, little brother. Now you’re talking.
Now you’re talking.” Quincy Jones, walking around like this, 12:08, Bel Air. Come on somebody. He
goes, “Storey, because they got one hit and they were satisfied and out. But there were more hits
underneath had they had the discipline to go look for them. You have success now, but if you only
knew what was under the surface. You are going from level, to level, to level, to level, because you
have the guts to dig deeper.” Clap your hands. You guys are on today.
[applause]
28:38 TS: Come on, come on! Say, I have uncommon thoughts. Say, I have uncommon discipline.
I’m gonna close with this. Now watch how cool this is. I saw this Kenyan guy on ESPN and man, this
dude had some Kenyan swag to him. So I was thinking, “Why this brother got all this Kenyan swag?”
So I started studying this thing about the Kenyans, that they have won 15 of the last 20 Boston
Marathons. Somebody pay attention. So this brother said, “When we get to Boston it’s easy for the
Kenyans. It’s like running downhill.” I’m like, “What?” So then he starts talking some more he says,
“The other runners do not train like the Kenyans. We train in high altitude on purpose, so when we

0:00:01 Jay Abraham: Why in the world would you possibly want to take a path going back to
mediocrity, when you can take the path to greatness?
0:00:09 Speaker 2: Jay Abraham, also known as the $9.4 billion man, the marketing legend of
legends, and the mentor to mentors. As the world’s highest paid marketing consultant, he has
significantly increased the bottom lines of over 10,000 clients in more than 400 industries world­wide.
0:00:31 Speaker 3: Using one of his principles, that we learned out of 200, helped skyrocket our
book “Chicken Soup for the Soul” to number one on The New York Times Bestseller List.
0:00:40 JA: I believe every human being was born to be great. I believe no human being wants to be
average. I believe no human being wants to be mediocre.
0:00:51 Speaker 2: Forbes magazine called him the “real thing” and lists Jay as one of the top five
executive coaches in the country. He is also a best­selling author and has been featured in USA
Today, The New York Times, Inc Magazine, and Success Magazine, among many others.
[music]
0:01:09 Daymond John: What’s up, everybody? This is Daymond John from ABC’s “Shark Tank.”
I’m a panelist and investor, also CEO of FUBU, and various other companies, and Presidential
Ambassador of Global Entrepreneurship. And I have to say that, at this point of my life, I probably
wouldn’t be here without my mentor, Jay Abraham. You may know him from his history as a legendary
marketer, or his theory on preeminence, or many of his books. And I have to say that through his
teachings and what he’s done for me, he’s taught me how to position myself in very unique positions.
What is my unique offering? As well as the ability to communicate, and then go after certain target
markets. He also finds other ways, and revenues, and areas that you could tap into your existent
customer base, and upsell, and provide even a better service. Also his ability to communicate and get
directly to the challenges at hand, and then find a way to solve it. It’s always great to have a solution.
But if you’re a doctor and you don’t have a diagnosis on the problem, then you can’t come up with a
solution. So I would have to say, if you have the good fortune of being in Jay Abraham’s presence,
if you have the ability to hear such a fast talker, who will keep your mind totally, totally thinking at
all times, you will absolutely benefit off of it in the greatest way. Life is a series of mentors and Jay
Abraham is absolutely the best in that area.
[music]

and see if he couldn’t find unrealized opportunities for these organizations, revenue opportunities,

0:06:56 JA: Okay, I’m gonna do a bloodless coup for about three minutes and tell you what I hope

lines of capitalism, solving problems, making over marketing, restructuring strategy, extricating people
from business Gordian knots, creating competitive advantages. And I’ve been very blessed to do
100s of industries, and see 1,000s of different ways to accomplish strategy, marketing, business.
0:07:46 JA: What has been very, very successful is getting people very clear on a problem, a
challenge, an issue, or an untapped opportunity. Having ‘em in the mic, giving them all of two or
three minutes max to pose the question, and we’ve given you a lot of background. The quality of your
question will determine the quality of the answer. I don’t have time, in this kind of an environment,
myself, and I don’t think Grant does either, to go probing for clarity. So you gotta be real clear: My
name is blank, here’s my business, here’s who I sell to, here’s what I sell, here’s how I sell, here’s
my problem. And if you can do that, between he and I, we’re gonna give you some kick ass, quick
answers. Our goal is to try to knock out 30, 40, 50 of you, as quickly as possible, so...
0:08:46 GC: Jay, let me ask you. In addition to time, your time, why is it important that they get clear
on the problem?
0:08:55 JA: That is a profound question, because, literally, if your assumption’s incorrect, everything
that flows from it’s incorrect. If you’re focused on the symptom and you don’t know the cause, then
you’re gonna pursue a bunch of tactical activities that will never get you anywhere, and there’s a great
statement. Most people, when they’re stressed or when they’re growing, dig holes.
The more stressed or the more excited they are, the deeper they dig the hole. The question is, first,
should you dig a hole? Second, if the answer is yes, should you dig it there? Third, if the answer is
yes or no, wherever you dig it, should you be digging it with a spoon or a power trowel? And third,
should you be the guy or the girl digging it? You gotta get clarity of answers. And my whole belief is
that every entrepreneur in this room needs to be a strategist, and most of you are tacticians.
0:09:55 GC: What does that mean? I don’t know what that means. Don’t use any big words for me.
I’m from the bayous of Louisiana, man.

0:10:01 JA: Okay, fair enough. You are expending your time, your effort, your opportunity, on
