masterful endgame plan. You don’t know what the endgame plan is, besides trying to make money
and stay in business. And so everything you do is a little bit of this, a little bit of that. There’s no
congruency. There’s no understanding of how to make everything keep paying off over and over and
over again.
0:10:37 Speaker 7: So which one are you talking about? The strategic or the tactician?

0:17:39 JA: I can tell you. Okay, it’s not just as simple. It’s realizing that everyone in this room has a
462

JAY ABRAHAM
different definition, a different impression, a different background. So any word you use, any strategy,
any directive, any concept you share, you’re coming from the palette of your mind and presuming
they’re interpreting it the same way. You’ve gotta get them to tell you what they heard, what they
think they’re supposed to do. When I go to a company, the first thing I do, is I interview 20 of the key
senior executives and I ask ‘em all the same questions. Their answers are all over the place. Let me
give you a good example. When I used to do seminars, I had a segment. I’d come back, purposely,
10 minutes late, and I would apologize, and I’d say, “Gee, I’m so sorry, but I saw the most stunning
woman out in the hall and I was captivated.” Then I’d come back and start my program. And then,
about half an hour later, I’d say, “You remember when I said that? Stand up, stand up, stand up, and
tell me what came to mind.” It was very interesting. A stunning woman to one person may be short,
voluptuous, European. To another, it’s tall, perky, blond. To a woman, who’s a little bit anti­sexist, it
might be somebody very conservative.
0:19:02 JA: And so no two people had the same definition, the same experiential base, the same
interpretation. I was telling somebody else, when we used to do seminars, we would stop at key
intervals. We’d have it set up differently. Everyone would go around a room or a table, explain
what they got out of it, and you’d hear, if you had 20 people at a table, you’d hear 19 different
interpretations. We’d try to figure out the most highest and universal, then we would share it with
everybody, because, no way, I don’t care, it’s not about them not listening. The flaw is you’re
predicating that they have the same values, the same vision, and the same definition. They don’t.
0:19:42 GC: Yeah. So is there a way, if you wanna go here, how do I get people to have the same
values that I have?
0:19:50 JA: Two ways and most people don’t do it. Number one, you literally start by expressing, not
the back row, but the literal, granular implications of your vision. I wanna have... Okay, may I divert for
a minute?
0:20:07 GC: Sure.
0:20:08 JA: Okay. I’ve done a lot of work on greatness. Done a lot of work on greatness. Every
human being is innately embedded with DNA for greatness. Greatness as a man, woman, husband,
wife, father, mother, lover, parent, employee, employer, and yet about 2% of the world becomes great.
Why? I have four answers. You wanna go on to something else?
0:20:33 GC: No, I love this.
0:20:34 JA: Okay. First answer, and this goes right to what Grant’s saying. “Nobody ever showed
them what greatness is supposed to be like internally, feel like emotionally, and in your soul,
expressed like, and be received like.” And then you’ve got all these different ways of being great:
Great employee, employer, leader. Entrepreneurs have to be leaders. So the first thing is figuring
what it looks like, and if you have five different areas of your life, what greatness is supposed to look
463

like as a husband, as a lover, as a friend, as a parent. When you get clarity on that, which almost
nobody gets, the next impact or constraint is, how do I get there? What path is gonna get me to, not
manipulated, strained, feigned, superficial greatness, but automatic greatness? Where it’s a natural
and a flowing thought coming out of your being? There’s lots of paths.
0:21:43 JA: Most people try to go that path and it’s like pole vaulting. When you find the shortest
path, now it becomes even more problematic. You’ve got to pursue it and anybody in this room that
either has or knows anybody that has children, little children, knows that when they try to talk, or walk,
or eat, or go poo­poo, they’re not very effective in the beginning, and unless they are supported and
championed by a parent, or a nanny, that keeps them encouraged and going forward, they would
never do it. It’s no different with a human being, except you need to paint pictures of what you think
it’s supposed to look like, and then you’ve gotta give them why. And why goes towards a lot of things.
Why goes to the corporate culture, but it also goes to the experiential outcome you want from the
recipient, your patient, your client. That’s [0:22:47]____first thing.
0:22:47 JA: Second, you’ve gotta give them a chance to play back what they got out of it, and what
they think you said, and what they think it means, and how they are going to execute. One other
point, that is very interesting, many people that are small, medium entrepreneurs complain to me
that they can’t hire quality people. And my answer is, “If you can’t hire them, grow and develop them,
because most people really aren’t invested in the growth and the development of a team.” When
you do that right, two things happen. Stats, research, you get 20% on average of the productivity of
a staff, team member, employee, whatever you wanna call it, not because they wanna be lazy, they
don’t know how to optimize. And if they don’t know, and you don’t help them, it’s a screw. You teach
masterable selling. What I find most hilarious, when you talk to most companies that have sales
people, I’ll say, “Okay. Do you train your people?” They’ll say, “Sure.” And I’ll say, “No, no, no. I’m not
saying you show them the products, the catalogues, the performance elements. Do you teach them
consultative selling? Do you teach them problem solving? Do you teach them Socratic interviewing?
Do you teach them how to listen and acknowledge? Do you teach them empathic selling?” They’ll go
like this. I could go on, but we’re gonna run out of time.
0:24:22 GC: Okay. You want to take a question?
0:24:24 JA: I do wanna take questions, but I wanna do it in a very meaningful way. So we’re gonna
ask this of you. You hopefully took the time to see the preface we gave you: Clarity, purity, distinction
of the question. If you are prepared to ask a great question, and the greatest ones will, frankly, be
ones that would be universally beneficial to everyone, that would be great. Go to the mic, about 20 of
you, and you’ll be given two minutes to pose your question. And then, I or Grant, I don’t care, will give
three minutes of answers. However, and this is me, not Grant. Grant is much more empathic, and
nicer, and loving, and kind, and considerate.

0:43:04 GM: My question is... It’s a personal question... Is what advice would you have for someone
who is looking to grow their business, but maybe, they’re a little bit impatient? They wanna take it to
the next level quick. But my problem is, right now, I wanna get there quicker, but it’s taking me longer
than I expected. Just impatient.
0:43:26 JA: Well, do you want to answer first, or you want... Well, I’ll tell you what I believe, ‘cause
I’ve done billions of dollars for people. When you have a good model that is solid, and solid means,
it’ll scale, if you bring other people in, it’s not gonna be marginal. What you want is to find people
who already have access to the influencers, or the entities, or the profile of people you want. And
you partner with them, because they’ve already got the trust, they got the influence. So you can do
organizations, you can do influencers, you can do people that sell candy stores, you can do people
that... I always figure out, “Who’s already got access to the market and who is not threatened by what
477

you do?” And we would have 30 different contacts. When we did the seminar business, I think I spent
$300,000 total, and we did $250 Billion, because we’d figured out who already had entrepreneurs,
who had professionals, magazines, Tony Robbins, investment newsletters. Before you do that, you
better make sure that your model makes sense and there’s enough in it to make it worthwhile for
them. Because if it isn’t, financially, and they’re not profoundly motivated to the charity, it’s gonna be a
waste. But that’s the safest, ‘cause they got the infrastructure...
0:44:52 GC: So, what he said, I’m his decipherer.
0:44:55 JA: That’s right, the UN, he’s my interpreter.
0:44:57 GC: Check it out. When Tim Story walked offstage here, I said, “Tim, you will never present a
$59 product ever again.”
[applause]
0:45:09 JA: “There’s no margin, Tim, for you, for others.” What he brought, the value he brought
here, is worth hundreds of times more than what he’s asking for his product. How many agree with
that?
[applause]
0:45:24 GC: So what Jay’s saying, Jay’s saying two things. You need to collaborate with people that

1:04:49 Chris Nagel: Mr. Cardone and Mr. Abraham, my name’s Chris Nagel, and I’m in the

them a results­based education system and one­on­one coaching. We market through digital media,
events, and also we have a TV show coming to a major network. Our biggest issue is also our biggest

maintain fulfillment, and how do we structure a strategic way to find new coaches and new strategic
partners to help us maintain fulfillment?
1:05:27 JA: Do you wanna answer?
1:05:28 GC: No.
1:05:28 JA: Okay, I’ll tell you how I would answer.
[laughter]
1:05:30 JA: No, it’s okay. I’ll tell you exactly what I would do. If you have a success process, that
491

you can prove, all you gotta do is go on LinkedIn and look for people that have worked for any of
the competitors, look for people that are in any of the related industries. And if you say, “We’ve got
a turn­key program. We can prove to you it works. All we need is the world’s top people to deliver
congruently,” and it’s not very hard, if you’ve got the proof. That’s what I’ve found. I always go to
people who either have worked for, or worked for, either direct or indirect competition, and I try to,
first, proof of concept. I don’t like to sell conjecture. I think selling conjectures, not only is it dangerous,
but it’s morally unfair to whoever you’re soliciting, because you can’t prove success. But if you’ve
already got a valid system, you can say, “Hey, I can deliver to you more clients than you can coach, if
you can deliver to me proof that you can coach at a stratospheric level of integrity and proficiency.” It’s
not really a problem. If you’ve got a model, just judging from what you’ve said, you have no problem.
You’ve got a great opportunity. That’s my answer.
1:06:48 GC: I would tell you that, having a bunch of coaches, with technology the way it is today...
Look, I used to travel all over the place delivering this stuff. Today, we will deliver my content. I can
control the content. I can control what’s in there. I can control what’s being shared, and today, it will
go out to probably 80,000 people. I know who they are, when they look at it, what they understand,
what they don’t understand, how often they look at it, when they turned it on, when they... Everything.
You cannot do that with human beings. You got 100 coaches out there, you’re trying to scale
the thing out, much less 200 or 300, you don’t know what they’re saying, when they’re saying it,
how the person actually liked it. It’s just a very difficult thing to duplicate. And with technology, I’m
using Lightspeed. Lightspeed and I have a partnership. With that kind of technology, dude, I know
everything about my client today, that Jay wanted me to know, when we sat you in the room.
1:07:51 JA: Yeah, that’s great, that’s great. No, it’s wonderful to hear two different, but convergent

If you study old­time optimization guys like Deming, they don’t want variation. Variation is the kiss of
death in any kind of a consistent performance environment, because one guy can make somebody a
fortune, the other can bury them. So I think that’s wonderful. Where’s Brad? Is Brad still here?
No?

it basically says, “You take the negative and you make it into as much of a positive as possible.” So
if you go to people and you say, “Look, buying a piece of real estate, you’re gonna live in, in another
place, or if it’s gonna be a rental, is a big decision, and I want you to know, that if I had the pockets,

Well, first thing, here’s what we do. And this is useful for all of you, in case we run out of time. When
I’m working with a client, we break the business into two parts. One, what they’re doing now, even
if it’s dumb, to generate revenue. Number two, what they’re not doing, that they could do better?
What they’re doing now, normally, is very underperforming, even if it’s not even an optimal marketing
revenue advertising approach, and you can normally increase its yield by 50% to 300%. So we do
that first, even if we’re not gonna keep it, because it throws off capital to reinvest in the higher, better
performing alternatives, that you either want to replace or add. All that stated, once we’ve done that,
I default, always, to figure out everybody who’s already got a relationship, everyone who’s already
selling something to that person, everyone who’s already got access, somebody who used worked
there. I look at LinkedIn. I looked at all the providers who sell to the same people. Who would take our
product on?
